title: Intro to the Platform & Welcome to the Course!

content.htmlContent:
Welcome to AI for Educators

Hello and a very warm welcome to the "AI for Educators" course! We're thrilled you're here to explore the transformative potential of Artificial Intelligence in the classroom.


At Academisthan, our core mission is to empower the architects of our nation's future—you. We've been in conversation with educators across India, and we see the ground shifting. The traditional chalk-and-talk model is giving way to a more dynamic, technology-infused environment. That's precisely why we created this course: not just to talk about change, but to give you the practical tools and confident mindset to lead it.

In a world that is evolving faster than ever, the roles of both teachers and students are changing, and this course is designed to empower you to lead that change with confidence and creativity.


Today's students, whether in K-12 or higher education, are digital natives preparing for a future we can only begin to imagine. They need more than facts; they need skills in critical thinking, creative problem-solving, and digital literacy. As educators, our role is shifting from being the primary source of information to becoming expert guides and facilitators of learning. This course was created to provide you with a practical toolkit to meet these new demands and harness the power of AI as a revolutionary teaching assistant.


How You Will Benefit from This Course

This program is designed to be hands-on and immediately applicable to your work. By the end of this course, you will be able to:

  • Build a Strong Foundational Understanding: You will master the "Big Four" of language models: ChatGPT, Google Gemini, Claude, and Microsoft Copilot. You'll learn the most critical skill of prompt engineering—how to ask AI the right questions to get the best results. We will also tackle the essential topics of ethics and responsibility, including how to navigate student use, data privacy, and AI bias.

  • Become an Efficient Content & Research Assistant: You will learn to save hours on prep work by using specialized tools. You will supercharge your research with Perplexity AI to find and cite academic sources , design complete, differentiated lesson plans with Eduaide.AI , and keep your entire digital workspace organized with Notion AI.

  • Create Stunning Visuals in Minutes: You will move beyond basic templates and learn to create professional-quality classroom materials effortlessly. This includes generating entire presentations from a single prompt with Gamma , designing posters and worksheets with Canva Magic Studio , and creating custom illustrations and diagrams using text-to-image tools.

  • Demystify AI Multimedia: You will learn how to create short video clips for lessons using RunwayML and generate audio versions of readings for greater accessibility using free text-to-speech tools.

  • Synthesize Your Skills into Powerful Workflows: The final module focuses on combining tools into efficient workflows. You will learn how to chain different AIs together—for example, researching a topic on Perplexity, outlining it in Notion AI, creating a presentation in Gamma, and then generating custom images in Canva. You will also learn how to automate professional communications by using AI to draft parent emails and newsletters.


This course is your opportunity to not only learn about AI, but to actively apply it in ways that enhance your teaching and enrich your students' learning experiences. Let's get started!

title: M1: Foundations: The Big Four of Language Models

content.htmlContent:
M1 - Learning Objectives

By the end of this module, educators will be able to:

  • Explain the fundamentals of Generative AI and Large Language Models (LLMs) in simple terms to students and colleagues.

  • Compare different types of AI tools (text-based, image-based, assessment tools, etc.) and evaluate when to use each in educational tasks.

  • Independently use at least two AI assistants (e.g., ChatGPT, Gemini AI, Claude, Copilot) to:

    • Generate lesson ideas and teaching materials

    • Draft emails, announcements, quizzes, and rubrics

    • Support student learning and differentiation

  • Apply effective prompting techniques to structure AI requests for accurate and classroom-ready outputs.

  • Review and refine AI-generated content to ensure subject accuracy, age appropriateness, and curriculum alignment.

  • Identify ethical considerations of AI usage in education (e.g., plagiarism, data privacy, bias) and apply practical classroom guidelines.

  • Use key AI terminologies confidently while discussing digital innovation in teaching and learning environment

steps.title: Welcome to the Future of Education

content.htmlContent:
Welcome to the Future of Education

You may have heard the popular saying: "AI will not replace teachers, but teachers who use AI will replace those who don't." While that statement can sound intimidating, we believe it's actually a message of incredible opportunity. The upcoming video will unpack this idea, shifting the focus from pressure to possibility. It will introduce AI not as another complex tool to learn, but as a powerful partner designed to enhance your skills, amplify your impact, and give you back your most valuable resource: time.


In the video, we will explore the fundamental "Why" behind integrating AI into your teaching practice, focusing on three core pillars. You will see practical demonstrations of how these tools can help you unlock time by streamlining lesson planning, boost your creativity by generating custom visuals instantly, and ultimately, deliver more dynamic engagement for your students. Get ready to see how this technology, which is available right now, can transform your workflow and redefine what's possible in your classroom.

The Three Pillars

As educators with countless responsibilities, the idea of learning new tools might feel overwhelming. But AI isn’t an extra task to add to your already full plate; it’s a way to transform how you approach every task.


In this section, we’ll explore three powerful pillars that define how AI can empower educators: Unlocking Time, Boosting Creativity, and Delivering Engagement.


1. Unlocking Time

For teachers, time is the most valuable and limited resource. Between lesson planning, grading, communicating with parents, and spending quality time with students, every minute counts.


AI helps you reclaim time by automating repetitive and time-consuming tasks. Imagine reducing your lesson-planning time by half, quickly generating high-quality worksheets, or designing presentations in minutes instead of hours.


With AI, you can shift your energy from administrative work to what matters most, connecting with and inspiring your students.


AI gives you back the time you need to teach, not just manage.


2. Boosting Creativity

Teachers are naturally creative thinkers. However, creativity is often limited by available tools, budget constraints, or lack of technical skills.


AI changes this completely - it becomes your creative co-pilot, helping you turn ideas into reality faster and more effortlessly.


From generating illustrations for your history class to designing interactive science visuals, AI allows you to create any scene or image you can describe. You’re no longer limited by what you can find online, you’re only limited by your imagination.


With AI, your classroom becomes a creative studio where ideas come to life instantly.


3. Delivering Engagement

Today’s learners live in a fast-paced, digital-first world. Capturing and holding their attention requires experiences that are not just informative but also immersive.


AI enables teachers to design dynamic and personalized learning experiences that resonate with students. By combining your educational expertise with AI’s efficiency and innovation, you can create content that not only captures attention but leaves a lasting impact.


AI helps you design engaging lessons that inspire curiosity and sustain attention.


AI is not here to replace educators, it’s here to empower them. By unlocking time, amplifying creativity, and elevating engagement, AI allows teachers to focus on what they do best: teaching, mentoring, and shaping the next generation of thinkers.

Generative AI

What is Generative AI?

Artificial intelligence (AI) is a transformative technology that holds immense potential to revolutionize the field of education. As educators, understanding the core concepts and applications of AI is crucial to effectively integrate this technology into modern classrooms. AI refers to the simulation of human intelligence processes by machines, including learning, reasoning, and problem-solving. In the context of education, AI can enhance teaching methods, personalize learning experiences, and streamline administrative tasks.


One of the key applications of AI in education is personalized learning. By analyzing vast amounts of data on student performance and engagement, AI systems can provide tailored recommendations and resources to meet individual learning needs. Teachers can utilize AI-powered tools to create adaptive learning pathways, allowing students to progress at their own pace and focus on areas where they need additional support. This personalized approach can lead to improved student outcomes and a more engaging learning environment.


Furthermore, AI can assist educators in automating routine tasks such as grading assignments, generating quizzes, and managing administrative duties. By delegating these time-consuming responsibilities to AI systems, teachers can allocate more time to provide personalized feedback, mentor students, and develop innovative teaching strategies. This not only increases efficiency in the classroom but also empowers teachers to focus on the human aspects of education that require empathy, creativity, and critical thinking.


Incorporating AI into teaching practices also opens up new possibilities for collaborative and interactive learning experiences. AI-powered tools like virtual reality simulations, intelligent tutoring systems, and chatbots can engage students in meaningful ways, encouraging active participation and deepening comprehension of complex concepts. By leveraging these technological advancements, teachers can create dynamic and immersive learning environments that cater to diverse learning styles and preferences.


However, it is important for educators to approach the integration of AI with a critical lens, considering ethical implications and potential biases that may arise from algorithmic decision-making. As AI continues to shape the landscape of education, teachers play a pivotal role in fostering digital literacy skills among students, encouraging ethical use of technology, and promoting a culture of lifelong learning and adaptability. By staying informed about the latest advancements in AI and embracing innovative teaching practices, educators can harness the power of this technology to empower their students and cultivate a future-ready mindset in the classroom.

 
 

Generative AI takes traditional artificial intelligence a step further by not just analyzing data, but actually creating new content. It can write essays or stories, generate images from a simple line of text, compose music, or even simulate human-like conversations. Unlike search engines that retrieve existing information, generative AI produces original content on the spot by learning patterns from massive amounts of data.


For educators, this means AI can become a powerful creative partner helping to design lessons, generate learning materials, and inspire new ways for students to engage with content. By understanding and leveraging generative AI, teachers can move beyond simply finding information to actively creating interactive, personalized, and innovative learning experiences.

Educational Impact

Understanding how generative AI works is only the first step. The real question is, what does this mean for teaching and learning? Technology is at its most powerful when it helps us connect with students in ways that were previously difficult or impossible. From tailoring lessons to individual needs, to breaking down language barriers, to fueling creativity in the classroom, AI has the potential to become a true partner in education. In this video, we’ll look at the educational impact of AI and explore how it can help us reimagine learning for every student.

 
 

We’ve just seen how AI can support learners by personalizing instruction, sparking creativity, and meeting students where they are. But to really understand this transformation, we need to zoom in on the powerful engines that make these capabilities possible. The upcoming video will introduce you to Large Language Models; the technology behind tools like ChatGPT and Gemini. We’ll break down what they are, how they work, and why they represent such a leap forward in the way we interact with information. Get ready to see how the “brain” behind generative AI actually functions, one prediction at a time.

Large Language Models

What are LLMs?

So far, we’ve explored what generative AI is and how it impacts education. But one question remains: what’s happening under the hood of the AI tools we use every day? When you type into ChatGPT or Gemini, there’s a powerful system working in the background that allows these tools to understand your words, follow context, and respond in natural language. These systems are called Large Language Models (LLMs). While the name may sound technical, the idea is surprisingly approachable. In this video, we’ll break down exactly what an LLM is and how it powers the conversations, summaries, and creative text we rely on.

 
 

Now that you understand how Large Language Models act as the “brain” behind text-based AI, the next step is to explore the tools built on top of them. Not all AI tools are the same, and knowing the differences can help you choose the right one for your needs. In the upcoming video, we’ll introduce the three types of AI tools: standalone apps you visit for specialized tasks, integrated features built directly into the software you already use, and powerful custom solutions designed for unique challenges. This clear framework will help you see the AI landscape more clearly and apply it more effectively in your own work.

Types of AI tools

By now, you’ve learned how AI works behind the scenes and what powers tools like ChatGPT or Gemini. But when it comes to actually using AI in daily life, the landscape can feel overwhelming. With so many apps, features, and platforms, how do you know which tool is right for the task at hand? That’s where a simple framework can make all the difference. In this video, we’ll break down AI tools into three easy-to-understand categories, helping you see the bigger picture and choose the right approach for your goals.

 
 

You now have a clear map of the AI landscape; standalone tools for specialized tasks, integrated features that work right inside your favorite apps, and powerful custom solutions for large-scale problems. But knowing the categories is just the beginning. The next step is to get to know the actual AI assistants you’ll be working with every day. In the upcoming video, we’ll introduce you to the “big four” assistants; ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and Copilot, each with their own strengths and specializations. Think of it as meeting your new team, ready to collaborate, brainstorm, and boost your productivity.

Meet Your AI Assistants

We’ve explored how AI works, the tools available, and the different ways they can be applied. But to really make AI useful in your daily work, it helps to think of these tools not as abstract technologies, but as teammates. Just like colleagues with different strengths; some great at designing, others at analyzing, others at sparking creativity, AI assistants each bring unique capabilities to the table. In this video, we’ll introduce you to the idea of AI as your extended team, ready to support you in planning, creating, and problem-solving.

 
 

Now that you’ve met the “team” of AI assistants, it’s time to get to know them individually, starting with one you’ve likely heard of or even used: ChatGPT. In the upcoming video, we’ll explore its strengths, from handling detailed conversations and writing support to generating ideas, translations, and even interactive classroom activities. You’ll also see practical demonstrations of how to use ChatGPT effectively in your workflow. Get ready to meet your first assistant in action.

ChatGPT

Artificial Intelligence has rapidly become an integral part of our professional and personal lives, and one of the most widely recognized tools in this space is ChatGPT. Whether you’re drafting content, brainstorming project ideas, learning a new topic, or simply exploring conversations in different languages, ChatGPT is designed to make the process easier, faster, and more engaging. In this video, we will take a closer look at how ChatGPT works, its key strengths, and practical use cases that can support learning, teaching, and everyday tasks. We’ll also explore its features step by step, so you can understand not just what it can do, but also how to get the best out of it.

 
 

Now that you’ve seen how ChatGPT can be used for writing, ideation, lesson planning, web searches, and even interactive conversations, you have a strong foundation to begin experimenting with it in your own work. Remember, the true value of ChatGPT lies in how effectively you frame your prompts and how creatively you apply its outputs.


In the next video, we’ll shift our focus to Google Gemini, another leading AI assistant. While it shares some similarities with ChatGPT, Gemini also brings unique features and integrations that make it stand out, especially in how it connects with the broader Google ecosystem. Exploring Gemini will give you a clearer picture of how different AI tools can complement each other and help you decide which one best fits your specific needs.

Google Gemini - Overview

This section introduces Google Gemini, a powerful, all-in-one AI platform designed for educators who want to go beyond text-based assistance. You’ll learn how Gemini supports deep research, handles multimedia, integrates across Google tools, and helps you create interactive and creative educational content with ease.


What is Google Gemini?

Gemini is Google’s advanced AI system that combines research, reasoning, and multimedia generation. Unlike simple chat-based AI tools, Gemini supports deep reasoning and can handle multiple content types - text, code, PDFs, images, and even videos.


Deep Reasoning & Research Capabilities

One of Gemini’s biggest strengths is its ability to perform in-depth analysis. It doesn’t just summarize, it can process large, detailed documents (like PDFs or research papers), extract key insights, and even interpret tables or visual data. This makes it a valuable co-pilot for academic research and project-based learning.


Handling Large Files and Multimedia

Gemini allows educators to upload large files such as PDFs, images, or video transcripts. This means you can analyze student essays, course readings, or lecture recordings without breaking them into smaller chunks. It can also interpret visual content, such as diagrams or slides, making it ideal for multidisciplinary subjects.


Integration with Google Workspace

Gemini’s standout feature is how smoothly it integrates with Google Docs, Sheets, and Slides. This allows you to:

  • Generate content in Gemini and transfer it directly into a Google Doc for editing.

  • Create lesson tables in Sheets or design presentations in Slides, all within a unified environment. This eliminates the need to constantly copy-paste or export between tools.


Gemini Flash vs. Gemini Pro

You’ll also learn about Gemini’s two versions:

  • Flash Mode: for quick answers, summaries, and light tasks.

  • Pro Mode (Gemini 2.5): for deeper reasoning, research, or complex, multi-step queries. Knowing when to use which mode helps you save time while still getting high-quality outputs.


Math, Coding & Interactive Learning Support

Gemini isn’t limited to text generation. It also supports math problem-solving, code writing, and even science simulations or guided learning activities. Teachers can use it to generate practice problems, create interactive lesson plans, or explore concept-based questions that combine logic and creativity.


“Gems”: Custom Mini-Tools

Just as ChatGPT has “Custom GPTs,” Gemini includes Gems, specialized versions tailored for specific tasks such as tutoring, translation, or creative writing. These Gems allow educators to personalize AI interactions to match specific classroom or subject needs.

 
 

Key Takeaways

  • Research & Reasoning Power: Gemini stands out for its ability to process large files, analyze data, and provide deep, well-reasoned insights.

  • Multimedia Compatibility: It supports multiple formats - text, images, videos, and code, helping you design diverse learning experiences.

  • Google Integration: Direct compatibility with Docs, Sheets, Slides, and Drive means instant access and seamless transitions between tools.

  • Multiple Modes: The Flash version is quick and lightweight, while the Pro version enables advanced reasoning and deeper analysis.

  • Creative Applications: Teachers can use Gemini for coding, STEM problem-solving, and creating interactive, visual learning content.


Now that you’ve seen what Gemini can do and how it fits into your teaching toolkit, let’s take a closer look at its key features in action.


In the next segment, we’ll explore Gemini’s interface, from file uploads and Gems to guided learning tools, and see how each feature supports real classroom applications.

Gemini AI Features

Every teacher and researcher has faced the same friction: you ask an AI a quick question, but the result is either too generic or you worry about its accuracy and provenance. What if the assistant could show how it arrived at an answer, let you refine that answer instantly, and export a ready-to-share document or email with one click?


Google Gemini not only generates answers, but also reveals its reasoning, offers versioned rewrites, verifies statements against web sources, and exports polished outputs directly into Google Docs or Gmail. This is about getting trustworthy, collaborative, and exportable AI outputs that integrate smoothly with your workflow.


Gemini is built to be both creative and evidence-aware. It interprets short prompts (for example, “features introduced in NEP”) and can infer context such as “NEP 2020.” More importantly, Gemini exposes several interaction layers that help you evaluate and use its output responsibly and efficiently:


Core Features Overview

  • Show thinking (explain its process): Gemini can display the steps it took to generate an answer (for example: search, sift official sources, analyze, consolidate). This transparency helps you judge how the response was formed.

  • Redo/Regenerate with versions: If you are not satisfied, Gemini can regenerate the response. It records different versions side-by-side so you can compare a concise paragraph form with a bulleted breakdown and choose the one you prefer.

  • Share conversation/Public links: You can create a shareable public link for the conversation or post directly to social platforms, enabling quick distribution of the AI’s output.

  • Export to Google Docs: Gemini can create a new Google Doc with the prompt as the title and the response as the body. This lets you collaborate, edit, and compile content without manual copy–paste.

  • Draft in Gmail: Gemini can paste the generated text into a new Gmail draft, saving time when you need to send an AI-created summary, report, or message.

  • Double-check response (evidence highlighting): Gemini evaluates its statements against Google Search and color-codes text: green indicates likely matching web content (linked), orange flags disagreement or low-match results (link if available), and no highlight means no match or non-factual text. Clicking a highlight reveals the supporting link and helps you verify claims.

  • Listen/Read-aloud: Gemini can read the response out loud, which helps when reviewing or presenting content.

  • Session organization (Recently uploaded/Watched/For you): Gemini surfaces recent uploads and personalized suggestions to streamline follow-up work.


Key Concepts

  • Inference of abbreviations and context: Gemini can infer meaning (e.g., understanding “NEP” as “NEP 2020”) so short prompts often work, but supplying explicit context still improves precision.

  • Process transparency (Show thinking): By showing its search and reasoning steps, Gemini makes AI outputs less of a black box and more auditable, useful for classroom citations and lesson prep.

  • Iterative refinement (Redo & Versions): Regenerating results is not just “try again.” Gemini refines scope and presentation (bullets vs. paragraph) so you get alternative styles without losing prior outputs.

  • Seamless export & collaboration: Export to Docs and Gmail turns single-session outputs into collaborative artifacts, ideal for team planning or sharing resources with students and colleagues.

  • Fact-check assistance (Double-check): The color-coded verification helps you distinguish between well-supported facts and statements that need further checking, reducing the risk of sharing unverified claims in class materials.


Practical Example Workflows

  • From prompt to lesson doc: Ask “List key features of NEP 2020.” Use Show Thinking to review sources. Redo to change tone or structure. Export to Google Docs, add school-specific notes, and share with the department.

  • Quick email to staff: Generate a summary of a policy update, Draft in Gmail, edit the subject, and send. No copy–paste needed.

  • Create evidence-backed handouts: Use the Double-check highlights to gather source links, then export those into a bibliography section of your lesson document.

  • Student-facing reading/audio: Use Listen to generate an audio version of the summary for visually impaired or auditory learners.


Benefits for Educators

  • Transparency: Show Thinking gives a clear audit trail for classroom citations.

  • Speed: Redo + Export features turn a prompt into a publishable doc or email in seconds.

  • Collaboration: Direct export to Google Docs integrates AI output with your existing collaborative workflows.

  • Reliability: Double-check helps flag statements that need verification before you share them with students.

  • Accessibility: Listen and export features make materials available in multiple formats quickly.


Why Integration Matters

Gemini’s features are designed to work together: you verify facts with Double-check, refine tone and structure with Redo, and then export the final output to Docs or Gmail for collaboration and distribution. This end-to-end flow reduces friction between drafting, checking, and sharing.

 
 

Gemini turns plain prompts into transparent, verifiable, and shareable outputs. Its Show Thinking feature makes reasoning visible, Redo and Versions let you compare styles, Double-check highlights provide quick evidence signals, and export options move content directly into Docs or Gmail for immediate collaboration.


Now that you understand these core capabilities, you are ready to apply Gemini to classroom materials that require polish and proof, like handouts, policy summaries, and staff communications.


In the next video, we will focus on using Gemini to create slide decks and structured reports. You will see how to prompt Gemini for slide outlines, speaker notes, data summaries, and visuals, and how to export those outputs into presentation tools for seamless delivery.

Google Gemini - Presentation & Reports

Every teacher needs clear, engaging lesson materials fast. Gemini converts a single prompt into: slide content plus speaker notes, suggested and generated images, short videos, and evidence-backed long reports. You will also see how Gemini reveals its reasoning, refines outputs, and exports them for immediate sharing or collaboration.


Gemini accepts a simple prompt (for example: “Create Grade 12 study notes on electricity with slides, images, and speaker notes”) and produces a multi-part package: slide text, speaker notes, image suggestions, and optional media (images/video). It supports iterative refinement (redo/versions), source-checking, and direct export to Docs, Gmail, sites, infographics, quizzes, or web pages.


Core Tools Overview

The platform’s key tools for presentations and reports include:

  • Slide + Speaker Notes generation: Gemini auto-creates slide content and matching speaker notes so teachers get both student-facing text and instructor prompts in one output.

  • Image suggestions & generation: For each slide Gemini recommends images (diagrams, photos, icons) and can generate those images on request, including labeled diagrams appropriate for classroom use.

  • Create images/Bulk image generation: You can ask Gemini to generate all images suggested across slides in one command, saving manual steps.

  • Video generation: Gemini can produce short explanatory videos (visuals, narration, sound effects, background music). You can specify length and focus for more precise results. Note that media generation takes longer than text.

  • Deep Research mode (Long-form reports): For reports, Gemini performs multi-stage research (broad search → refine → gather data → synthesize), documents its thinking, and compiles evidence-backed, structured reports with sources, tables, charts, and concluding analysis.

  • Export & publish: Outputs can be exported directly to Google Docs, Gmail drafts, Google Sites, or rendered as infographics and web pages—no copy–paste required.

  • Interactive quiz creation: Gemini can auto-generate quizzes from a report or uploaded notes and make them interactive inside the platform.

  • Versioning & redo: Gemini keeps alternative versions (bulleted vs. paragraph, different tones) so you can compare and pick the best structure instantly.

  • Evidence highlighting/provenance: Gemini highlights statements with links and color-codes how well web sources support each claim, helping you vet material before classroom use.

  • Listen/Read-aloud: Gemini can read generated content aloud for review or accessibility.


Benefits for Educators

  • Speed: Create polished presentations, media, and reports far faster than manual assembly.

  • Accuracy & transparency: Source highlights and documented research steps reduce the risk of sharing unverified claims.

  • Consistency: Speaker notes and slide text stay aligned, improving lesson delivery.

  • Accessibility: Read-aloud and auto-generated visuals support diverse learners.

  • Shareability: Direct export integrates with existing classroom workflows (Docs, Slides, Gmail, Sites).


Why Integration Matters

Gemini’s tools are designed to flow: draft content, verify sources, refine structure, generate images/video, then export for collaboration or publication. That single workflow reduces friction between planning, teaching, and assessing.

 
 

Gemini turns a single instructional prompt into a complete teaching package: slides with speaker notes, classroom-ready images, videos, long evidence-backed reports, and interactive quizzes. Its transparency, versioning, and export features make it practical for lesson prep, research briefs, and student-facing resources.


In the next video, we will compare how Claude approaches creative teaching tasks, classroom artifacts, and large-document workflows, focusing on template creation, interactive lesson artifacts, and privacy-forward classroom features.

Claude - Introduction

Artificial Intelligence tools are increasingly becoming indispensable partners in education, helping teachers design lessons, organize content, and create interactive learning experiences. In this session, you will be introduced to Claude, an advanced AI assistant developed by Anthropic.


Claude can be thought of as your creative and research-oriented co-teacher. While tools like ChatGPT or Gemini are versatile generalists, Claude excels in creativity, large document handling, and generating structured educational content. It is particularly useful for educators, researchers, and curriculum designers who need to work with extensive material or want to create customized resources quickly.


Here are some of the key strengths of Claude you’ll learn about in this video:

  • Accurate and detailed research: Claude generates well-organized responses with fewer factual errors.

  • Data privacy and safety: It prioritizes privacy and ensures content is suitable for safe classroom use.

  • Multi-input capability: It can process both text and images, making it versatile for different teaching tasks.

  • Large document handling: Claude can read, summarize, and analyze lengthy essays, reports, or even curriculum documents efficiently.


For educators, Claude’s most powerful feature is its ability to help with curriculum and lesson planning. It allows you to design, adapt, and customize lessons for different subjects and student groups. For instance, teachers can use Claude to:

  • Develop detailed, time-saving lesson plan templates.

  • Provide personalized student feedback.

  • Create interactive learning activities like group discussions or quizzes.

  • Adapt lessons based on student needs and learning styles.


In the video, you will also explore Claude’s interface, particularly the Artifact feature, which stands out as one of its most useful tools for teachers. Artifacts allow users to create interactive and reusable documents, such as lesson plans, templates, and assignments, directly within the Claude workspace.


Through a step-by-step example, you will see how a teacher creates a reusable, editable lesson plan template using Claude’s Artifacts.


The session also demonstrates how Claude allows teachers to add interactive features, like a drag-and-drop section for collaborative classroom activities (e.g., debates, role plays, think-pair-share, and group discussions). This makes lesson plans dynamic and adaptable for daily teaching.

 
 

Claude AI can act as a true creative assistant for teachers and educators. Its Artifact feature allows you to design structured templates, organize your lessons, and even include interactive elements without needing any coding or design expertise.


By using Claude for tasks like lesson design, reflection notes, and classroom activities, educators can focus more on teaching and student engagement rather than administrative planning.

Claude - Interactive Learning Artifacts Spotlight

Imagine creating an interactive classroom activity - flashcards, a chemistry simulation, or a student newsletter, without switching between multiple apps. You simply describe what you want, and an intelligent assistant builds it for you.


That’s what Claude’s “Artifacts” make possible. These are interactive, ready-to-use tools that teachers and students can generate and customize instantly, from visual learning aids to creative classroom resources.


In this spotlight, we’ll explore the power of Interactive Learning Artifacts, and how they can transform the way you teach and learn.


Here’s what you’ll discover:

  • Molecular Studio: Visualize and explore 3D molecular structures.

  • Language Tutor: Practice languages with real-time feedback.

  • Weekly Newsletter Generator: Create classroom newsletters automatically.

  • Creative & Coding Tools: Generate activities, brainstorm ideas, and more.


Molecular Studio

This feature turns complex chemistry lessons into visual, hands-on exploration.


Simply type the name of a molecule, for example, “glucose”, and the AI generates a 3D molecular model. You can rotate, zoom, and interact with it to explore the structure’s atoms and bonds.


When you try another example, like “water,” it instantly updates with the correct formula (H₂O), atom count, and molecular bonds, making abstract concepts tangible.


Language Tutor

This artifact is like having a personal AI language coach. You type a sentence, for example, “Hi, how are you?”, and the tool instantly corrects and translates it into your target language, such as Spanish. It not only provides the correct phrase (“Hola, ¿cómo estás?”) but also gives feedback on vocabulary, grammar, and accuracy.


Creative & Productivity Tools

Artifacts aren’t just for academic subjects. They also simplify creative and classroom management tasks. You’ll find tools like:

  • QR Code Generator: Instantly create links to resources or class projects.

  • Brainstorm & Idea Generator: Spark creativity for group projects or essays.

  • Writing Editor: Refine tone, grammar, and structure of assignments.

  • Email Assistant: Draft messages to parents or staff efficiently.

  • Meeting Notes & Summaries: Capture highlights from student meetings.

  • Team Activity Ideas: Generate quick activities for group learning sessions.


Weekly Newsletter Generator

This feature shows how AI can enhance classroom communication.


When you type a prompt like: “Create a weekly newsletter for the classroom on the activities completed and contributions from students,” the AI automatically builds a “Classroom Chronicle”, a structured, editable newsletter that includes:

  • Week’s activities (organized by day)

  • Student spotlights and achievements

  • Upcoming classroom events

  • Teacher contact details and class announcements


Games & Interactive Creativity

Artifacts also include playful tools that combine coding, creativity, and music, such as “Piano Way of Code.” These allow students to learn logic and programming through interactive, game-like environments.

 
 

You’ve now explored Claude’s newest educational innovation - Interactive Learning Artifacts.


What once took hours of planning or specialized tools can now be done in minutes. Whether you’re designing visuals, creating newsletters, or guiding student practice, these features give you the freedom to focus on what truly matters - teaching, connecting, and inspiring.

steps.title: Claude - Interactive Assignment Brief Template Generator

content.htmlContent:
Claude - Interactive Assignment Brief Template Generator

In this section, you will explore how AI can help educators design interactive, reusable templates that simplify academic processes such as creating assignment briefs, project outlines, and evaluation forms. You’ll see how Claude, an advanced AI tool can generate structured, professional templates with editable fields in minutes, saving hours of manual formatting work.


Artifact Creation Through AI

Claude allows teachers to generate artifacts, educational documents, forms, or templates that can be reused and shared across courses. By giving clear prompts, you can instruct the AI to include the sections, tone, and level of formality you need.


Structured Prompting for Template Design

When designing a standardized Assignment Brief Template, the teacher specifies:

  • Course and department details

  • Learning outcomes and core concepts

  • Task instructions and deliverables

  • Submission method and referencing style

  • Rubric, evaluation criteria, and feedback fields


Each of these inputs helps the AI automatically structure an editable, user-friendly form.


Interactivity in AI-Generated Templates

Claude doesn’t just produce plain text. It can create interactive HTML templates with clickable fields, dropdown menus, and checkboxes. For example:

  • A dropdown menu for “Submission Method” (Online, Email, Hard Copy)

  • Editable boxes for “Learning Outcomes” or “Grading Rubric”

  • Auto-generated placeholders for course name, title, and due date


This transforms a static document into a dynamic, ready-to-use resource for any department.


Department-Wide Standardization

One of the biggest challenges in higher education is inconsistency across departments. With AI-generated templates, administrators can ensure every faculty member uses a consistent format that meets institutional standards while still allowing subject-specific customization.


AI as a Collaborative Design Partner

Rather than replacing human creativity, Claude functions as a collaborative assistant, helping educators structure, format, and streamline their teaching documentation. The teacher defines what is needed; Claude handles how it is presented.

 
 

Key Takeaways

  • AI as a Document Designer: Claude can generate structured, editable templates that make institutional documentation easy, consistent, and professional.

  • Prompts Shape Quality: The detail in your prompt determines the quality of your output. Specific fields, formats, and tone instructions lead to precise and polished templates.

  • Efficiency and Scalability: Once created, templates can be reused by all departments, ensuring uniformity in communication and expectations.

  • Interactive Capabilities: Claude supports dynamic features like fillable boxes, dropdowns, and grading rubrics, transforming static files into interactive resources.

  • Empowering Educators: The tool doesn’t just save time. It enhances quality and allows educators to focus on pedagogy, not paperwork.


Now that you’ve seen how Claude helps educators design structured templates from scratch, we’ll explore Microsoft Copilot, a tool that integrates AI directly into your everyday apps like Word, PowerPoint, and Excel.

Microsoft Co-Pilot

Microsoft Copilot is an AI assistant built by Microsoft. It uses OpenAI’s GPT (Generative Pretrained Transformer) technology to help users write, think, and work faster.


Copilot is integrated across Microsoft products such as Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, and Teams. It acts like a smart digital helper that understands natural language, meaning you can talk to it or type instructions just as you would to a human assistant.


The goal of Microsoft Copilot is to help people do more with less effort. Instead of spending time on repetitive or routine tasks, users can focus on creativity, analysis, and decision-making.


For example:

  • In Word, Copilot can help you draft reports or rewrite content.

  • In Excel, it can summarize data or create charts.

  • In PowerPoint, it can design slides from a short prompt.

  • In Outlook, it can help write and organize emails.

  • In Teams, it can summarize meeting notes or generate action items.


How Copilot Works

Microsoft Copilot is powered by large language models (LLMs), specifically versions of OpenAI’s GPT-4. It also connects to Microsoft Graph, which is a system that stores data from your Microsoft 365 account (emails, files, calendars, chats, etc.).


Here’s how it works in simple steps:

  1. You give a prompt like “Summarize this document” or “Create a presentation on classroom AI tools.”

  2. Copilot reads your input and searches your data (if you allow it) using Microsoft Graph.

  3. It uses its language model to generate a smart and relevant response.

  4. You can edit, accept, or reject the result. You always stay in control.


This combination of GPT + Microsoft Graph allows Copilot to give personalized and context-aware assistance.


Where You Can Find Microsoft Copilot

Microsoft Copilot appears in many familiar tools educators already use:

  • Word Copilot: Write lesson plans, summaries, and feedback.

  • Excel Copilot: Analyze student data, attendance, or grades quickly.

  • PowerPoint Copilot: Turn lesson ideas into slides automatically.

  • Outlook Copilot: Manage inboxes, write parent emails, and draft newsletters.

  • Teams Copilot: Summarize meetings, plan events, and capture decisions.

  • Copilot Chat (Web & Windows): A chat-style version where you can ask questions, brainstorm ideas, or generate quick information.


There’s also Copilot in Edge and Bing, which helps with online research, web summaries, and lesson resource collection.


Copilot for Educators

For teachers, Copilot can save time and reduce workload. 


Here are some real classroom uses:

  • Lesson Planning: “Create a 40-minute lesson on photosynthesis for Grade 8.”

  • Assessment Support: “Generate short answer questions from this chapter.”

  • Parent Communication: “Draft an update email about the upcoming field trip.”

  • Student Feedback: “Write constructive feedback for a student struggling with grammar.”

  • Data Analysis: “Summarize student performance trends from this spreadsheet.”


By using Copilot, educators can focus more on teaching and mentoring rather than repetitive administrative tasks.


Privacy and Safety

Microsoft Copilot is designed with data security and privacy in mind. All data stays within the Microsoft 365 environment. That means your school or institution controls its data. It's not used to train public AI models. Copilot also follows the same compliance standards as Microsoft 365, which makes it suitable for educational and organizational use.


Advantages of Microsoft Copilot

Feature

What It Means for Educators

Integration

Works inside Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Teams, and Outlook; no extra app needed.

Context Awareness

Understands your documents, emails, and schedules using Microsoft Graph.

Time-Saving

Reduces hours spent on administrative work.

Personalization

Adapts to your teaching style and tone.

Data Security

Keeps your institutional data private and compliant.

Getting Started

To start using Microsoft Copilot:

  1. Make sure you have a Microsoft 365 Education license that includes Copilot.

  2. Open any Microsoft app (like Word or PowerPoint).

  3. Look for the Copilot icon (a small colorful circle).

  4. Click it and type your question or command. For example:

    • “Create a 5-slide presentation on climate change.”

    • “Summarize this document for a quick briefing.”


Copilot will generate a response that you can adjust or build upon.


Key Takeaway

Microsoft Copilot is not just a tool, it’s a teaching partner. It helps educators save time, create faster, and teach smarter. By learning to use it effectively, teachers can focus on what truly matters: helping students learn and grow.

M1 - Task 2

Comparing Your AI Assistants

Objective: The objective of this task is to give you a hands-on understanding of how different Large Language Models (LLMs) respond to the same prompt. You will discover that each AI has a unique style, tone, and structure. This exercise will help you begin to identify which assistant might be best suited for different educational tasks.


Instructions:

  1. Choose Your Tools: Select three of the four AI assistants you set up in the previous task (e.g., ChatGPT, Google Gemini, Claude, or Microsoft Copilot).

  2. Select a Prompt: Choose one or two prompts that you would like to experiment with for your area of interest.

  3. Run the Experiment: Copy your chosen prompt and paste the exact same text into each of the three AI assistants you selected.

  4. Compare the Outputs: Read and compare the three unique responses that you receive. Pay attention to differences in tone, formatting, length, and the overall quality of the content.


Reflection Questions

  1. Prompt & Tools: Which prompt did you choose, and which three AI assistants did you use?

  2. Key Differences: Briefly summarize the main differences you observed between the three responses. Consider things like:

    • Tone: (e.g., formal, creative, conversational)

    • Format: (e.g., bullet points, numbered lists, paragraphs)

    • Content: (e.g., Did one provide more creative ideas? Was one more concise?)

  3. Most Useful Response: Which of the three responses did you find most useful for your needs as an educator, and why?

  4. Least Useful Response: Was there a response you found less helpful? If so, why?


Please mark this task as 'Complete' in the LMS to proceed to the next activity.

The Art of Prompting (CPTF)

You are about to explore one of the most crucial skills for leveraging Artificial Intelligence in education: The Art of Prompting.


Think of AI as a brilliant, infinitely knowledgeable, and tirelessly creative assistant. It can draft lesson plans, create differentiated learning materials, explain complex topics in simple terms, and even generate ideas for classroom activities. However, like any powerful assistant, it needs clear and precise instructions to deliver its best work. Simply asking a question is not enough.


This is where prompting comes in.


Why Does Prompting Matter? From a Vague Wish to a Powerful Tool

The quality of the output you get from an AI is a direct reflection of the quality of the input you provide. A vague request will almost always lead to a generic, uninspired, or even unhelpful response. An effective prompt, on the other hand, can unlock truly transformative results.


Consider the difference:

Example 1: 

Vague prompt: "Create a quiz on World War II."

Effective Prompt: "Create a 5-question multiple-choice quiz on the key turning points of World War II for a 10th-grade history class. The questions should assess understanding of events like the Battle of Stalingrad and D-Day. Provide an answer key with a brief explanation for each correct answer."


Example 2 : 

Vague Prompt:  “Explain photosynthesis”

Effective Prompt:  "Explain the process of photosynthesis using an analogy of a solar-powered factory. Your target audience is a 7th-grade science class. Please define the terms 'chlorophyll', 'glucose', and 'stomata' in simple language within your explanation."


As you can see, the second prompt doesn't just ask a question; it provides context, constraints, and a clear goal. This is the essence of effective prompting.


The Goal: Moving from AI User to AI Architect

Learning to prompt well is the difference between being a passive user of AI and becoming an architect of AI-powered learning experiences. For an educator, this skill is a superpower. It allows you to:

  • Save Hours of Time: Automate the creation of rubrics, emails, study guides, and administrative documents.

  • Enhance Personalization: Instantly generate reading passages at different Lexile levels or create math problems tailored to specific student needs.

  • Boost Creativity: Brainstorm innovative project ideas, role-playing scenarios, or debate topics that ignite student curiosity.

  • Simplify Complexity: Break down abstract scientific theories or historical events into digestible, relatable content for your students.


Just like you use pedagogical frameworks to structure your lessons, there are prompting frameworks to structure your conversations with AI. In the upcoming video, we will move beyond simple questions and introduce you to structured approaches like CPTF, RACE, and concepts like Zero-Shot and Few-Shot prompting.


These frameworks are not technical jargon; they are practical recipes for success. They provide a reliable blueprint to ensure you give the AI everything it needs to generate precisely what you want, every single time.


Before you press Play on the next video, take a moment to reflect on a task you do regularly that you wish you could speed up or improve. Keep that task in mind as you learn how to craft the perfect prompt to make it happen.

M1 - Task 3

Create a detailed prompt for a lesson plan idea using a specific framework (e.g., CPTF)


Objective: Now that you've seen how different AIs respond to simple prompts, it's time to take control. The objective of this task is to apply a structured prompting framework to engineer a high-quality, specific output from an AI. By using a framework, you move from making a request to giving a professional brief, resulting in content that is far more useful and tailored to your exact needs.


The CPTF Framework

In the video lesson, we introduced prompting frameworks. For this task, we will use the CPTF framework. As a reminder, here are its components:

  • C - Context: Provide the background, the "need-to-know" information for the AI. Who are the students? What have they already learned?

  • P - Persona: Tell the AI who it should be. Should it act as a curriculum designer, a subject matter expert, a creative storyteller?

  • T - Task: This is the specific, actionable thing you want the AI to do. Be clear and direct.

  • F - Format: Define the structure of the output. Do you want a table, bullet points, a numbered list, specific headings?


Instructions:

  1. Choose Your Topic: Select a real topic for a lesson plan that you would teach in your own classroom.

  2. Build Your Prompt: In a personal document or notebook, write out each of the four CPTF sections for your chosen topic. Use the example below as a guide.

  3. Combine and Refine: Merge your four sections into a single, comprehensive paragraph. This will be your final prompt.

  4. Test Your Prompt: Paste your final prompt into the AI assistant of your choice (ChatGPT, Gemini, or Claude) and review the response it generates.

  5. Reflect and Complete: Consider the self-reflection question below. Once you have completed the practice, you can mark this task as complete.


Checklist & Self-Reflection:

For this practice, ensure you have completed the following steps in your own notes:

  • Your CPTF Breakdown: Each of the four sections written out.

  • Your Final Combined Prompt: The single paragraph you pasted into the AI.

  • Review the AI's Output: Read through the complete lesson plan generated by the AI.

  • Self-Reflection Question: Ask yourself, "How did using a framework change the quality of the AI's output compared to a simple prompt?"


Once you are finished, please mark this activity as 'Complete' in the LMS.

Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Prompting

Mastering different prompting techniques is the key to transforming an AI from a simple search engine into a useful teaching and research assistant. Understanding zero-shot and few-shot prompting allows you to control the depth, tone, and format of AI-generated content.


Zero-Shot Prompting

What it is: This is the most direct method. You give the AI a task without providing any prior examples of the desired output. You are leveraging the AI's extensive training to execute a request based on the instruction alone.


Analogy: You ask an expert research assistant to "find and summarize key papers on post-colonial theory." You trust their expertise to handle the task effectively.


When to use it: For well-defined academic or administrative tasks where the desired output is straightforward. It is highly efficient for brainstorming, drafting, and summarizing.


Use Cases:

  • Developing Course Content and Ideas:

    • Prompt: "Generate five thought-provoking opening hooks for an undergraduate lecture on the ethical implications of CRISPR technology."

    • Prompt: "Brainstorm potential research questions for a postgraduate seminar on the topic of sustainable urban development in South Asia."


  • Drafting Professional Communications:

    • Prompt: "Draft a professional email to the department head arguing for the adoption of a new statistical software package for our research methods course."

    • Prompt: "Write a concise announcement for the university's Learning Management System (LMS) informing students of a revised deadline for their final term paper."


  • Summarizing and Explaining Complex Theories:

    • Prompt: "Summarize the core arguments of this academic paper on behavioral economics into five key bullet points for a pre-reading guide." (After pasting the paper's text).

    • Prompt: "Explain the concept of 'hegemony' as described by Antonio Gramsci, in a way that is accessible to a second-year undergraduate political science class."


Few-Shot Prompting

What it is: A more precise technique where you provide the AI with a few examples (the "shots") of the desired output before giving the final task. This trains the AI on the specific style, format, and nuance you require.


Analogy: Instead of just asking your research assistant for a summary, you provide them with two summaries you've written previously and say, "Summarize these new papers in this exact format and style."


When to use it: When you need a highly specific, customized, or consistently formatted output that the AI might not produce on its own. It is essential for assessment design, data formatting, and nuanced analysis.


Use Cases for University Lecturers and Professors:

  • Creating Custom Assessment Materials:

    • Prompt: Here are examples of how I write my short essay exam questions:

      • Topic: The French Revolution -> Question: "Analyze how the 'Reign of Terror' both supported and undermined the original goals of the French Revolution."

      • Topic: Plate Tectonics -> Question: "Discuss the evidence supporting the theory of plate tectonics and explain its implications for understanding global seismic activity."

    • Now, create a question in the same style for this topic:

      • Topic: Shakespeare's Hamlet


  • Analyzing Qualitative Data or Student Feedback:

    • Prompt: Categorize this student feedback from end-of-semester reviews as 'Praises Specifics', 'General Praise', 'Actionable Suggestion', or 'Confused'. See examples:

      • Comment: "The lecture on cellular mitosis was fantastic." -> Category: General Praise

      • Comment: "I think the course would be improved by adding a weekly, ungraded quiz to help us review." -> Category: Actionable Suggestion

      • Comment: "The case study on the Enron scandal really clarified the concept of corporate governance." -> Category: Praises Specifics

    • Now, categorize the following feedback:

      • "I'm still not clear on the difference between correlation and causation."

      • "It would be great if the lecture slides were posted 24 hours in advance."

      • "This was one of my favorite courses."


  • Matching a Specific Pedagogical Style:

    • Prompt: Rewrite this technical sentence into an accessible, inquiry-based question for students.

      • Original: "The proliferation of digital communication platforms has fundamentally altered the paradigms of political discourse." -> Rewritten: "How has the rise of social media changed the way politicians communicate with the public and how we discuss politics?"

    • Now, rewrite this sentence in the same inquiry-based style:

      • Original: "The novel's use of an unreliable narrator forces the reader to question the veracity of the events being described."

 Key Advantage

Zero-Shot

 For general academic and administrative tasks: brainstorming research topics, drafting emails, summarizing articles, getting initial ideas.

Speed and Efficiency

Few-Shot

For specialized academic tasks: designing exam questions, analyzing student feedback, creating content in a specific format or pedagogical style.

Precision and Control

steps.title: Ethics & Responsibility in AI Enabled Classrooms

content.htmlContent:
Ethics & Responsibility in AI Enabled Classrooms

Plagiarism & Assignments

Has AI ended traditional assignments, or has it given us a reason to redesign them for deeper learning?


That’s the question every educator is grappling with today. But perhaps the real issue isn’t about stopping students from using AI. It’s about guiding them to use it meaningfully. Rather than viewing AI as a threat to academic integrity, what if we saw it as an opportunity to strengthen critical thinking, creativity, and authentic learning?


In this video, we're flipping the script. We'll show you how to design "AI-resistant" assignments and shift the focus from the final product to the learning process.


Ready to move from anxiety to action? Let's build the user, not ban the tool.

 
 

As we've seen, the most effective way to foster academic integrity is through thoughtful pedagogy and assignment design. By focusing on the learning process, the drafts, the personal reflections, the unique connections, we naturally build a classroom culture where AI is a tool, not a shortcut.


However, verification still plays a crucial role. This is where tools like Turnitin, Copyscape, and other AI detectors become relevant. These platforms are no longer just for checking traditional plagiarism; they are evolving to identify text that has the statistical patterns of AI generation.


It's critical, though, to approach these tools with the right mindset:

  • They are a compass, not a judge. An AI detection score is an indicator that can prompt a conversation with a student about their writing process. It is not definitive proof of misconduct.

  • False positives can happen. These tools are not perfect. Their results should be combined with your own professional judgment and your understanding of the student's previous work.

  • They support, not replace, good teaching. The strategies covered in this video remain your primary defense. These tools are a backstop for the rare cases where you have concerns.


Think of these detectors as one part of a larger conversation about digital literacy and honesty, a conversation you are now fully equipped to lead.

Data Privacy & AI Bias

In our previous discussions, we focused on what students can create with AI. Now, we'll explore the other side of the interaction: what students give to AI and the hidden biases they might encounter.


Every prompt a student writes is a piece of data. Where does it go? Is their personal information or academic work secure? Furthermore, the answers they get back aren't always neutral. AI learns from the internet, and the internet is full of human stereotypes and skewed perspectives.


This video will equip you with the knowledge to guide your students through these critical issues. You will learn about:

  • Data Privacy: Understanding the risks of sharing Personally Identifiable Information (PII) and the concept of "prompt hygiene."

  • AI Bias: Seeing a live demonstration of how AI can produce stereotypical results and why it happens.

  • The Educator's Role: Practical strategies for turning these challenges into powerful, teachable moments on digital citizenship and critical thinking.


Press play to learn how to become the essential "human in the loop" who can help students use AI safely and critically.

 
 

As you just saw, our responsibility as educators extends beyond teaching how to use AI; we must also teach how to use it wisely. By understanding data privacy and recognizing bias, we can empower students to be critical, safe, and ethical digital citizens. The goal is not to fear the tool, but to understand its limitations and guide students accordingly.


Your Key Takeaways:


1. On Data Privacy - Teach "Prompt Hygiene":

  • Advise students to treat public AI tools like a public space. They should never input sensitive Personally Identifiable Information (PII) like their full name, school, address, or personal anecdotes.

  • Favor institution-approved tools whenever possible, as they often have better data protection policies.

  • Instruct students not to paste entire essays or proprietary work into public tools, as that content can be used to train future models.


2. On AI Bias - Use it as a Teachable Moment:

  • AI is a mirror reflecting the data it was trained on, biases and all. The "CEO" and "nurse" examples are perfect for starting classroom discussions.

  • Teach students to question the output. Encourage them to ask: "Whose perspective is missing?" or "Is this a stereotype?"

  • Promote better prompting. Show students that by being more specific in their prompts (e.g., "Create an image of a female CEO from India"), they can guide the AI toward more diverse and accurate results.


Your Next Steps (Reflection):

Think about an upcoming project or assignment in your class.

  1. How could you add a simple instruction or a brief discussion about "prompt hygiene" to ensure students protect their data?

  2. Could you design a short activity where students deliberately test an AI for bias (like the examples in the video) and then reflect on the results?


By proactively teaching these concepts, you are equipping your students with essential skills for navigating not just the classroom, but the world.

Setting Classroom Policy

In today's classrooms, AI is no longer a futuristic concept, it's a reality. Students are already using tools like ChatGPT, Google Gemini, and Perplexity AI for a variety of tasks, from brainstorming and outlining to simplifying complex topics and fact-checking. 


This trend highlights a major shift: AI is becoming an essential part of students' academic lives. However, this also brings up important ethical considerations. For instance, what's the difference between using AI as a thinking partner for generating ideas versus using it to plagiarize an entire assignment? How can we teach students to critically evaluate AI-generated information, which can sometimes be biased or factually incorrect?


In this video, we'll shift our focus from these individual concepts to a single, actionable strategy: creating a clear and simple AI usage policy for your classroom. We'll explore the key components of such a policy and how you can tailor it to your students and subject matter, ensuring they use AI ethically and responsibly.

 
 

Now, it's your turn to put these concepts into practice.


Take some time to think about the following:

  • Your Subject and Students: What specific subjects or grade levels do you teach? How might AI be uniquely helpful or challenging in that context? For example, in a history class, AI could be used to summarize historical documents, but students must be taught to critically evaluate potential biases in the AI's output. In a creative writing class, AI could be a brainstorming partner, but students must be transparent about its use and focus on developing their own unique voice.

  • Your Learning Goals: What are the core learning objectives for your course or classroom? How can an AI policy support these goals rather than hinder them? For instance, if a learning goal is to develop critical thinking, you might design an assignment where students use AI to generate a first draft and then spend the bulk of their effort fact-checking, revising, and identifying biases in the AI's work.

  • A Starting Point: Using the three components from the video, permitted uses, prohibited uses, and citations, draft a simple, first-version policy for your class. Remember, this doesn't have to be perfect. The goal is to create a starting point that you can refine over time.


Once you have a draft, consider sharing it with a colleague or discussing it with your department. Collaboration can help you refine your policy and ensure it aligns with broader school or institutional guidelines.

title: M2: The Educator's Content & Research Assistant

content.htmlContent:
M2 - Learning Objectives

By the end of this module, educators will be able to:

  • Use Perplexity AI to conduct academic research beyond traditional search engines.

  • Analyze and cite credible academic sources using the Focus feature.

  • Create complete lesson plans with Eduaide.AI, including objectives, teaching strategies, and resource suggestions tailored to grade level and learning outcomes.

  • Develop different learning materials such as simplified text, extension activities, varied learning modalities) using Eduaide’s content generation tools.

  • Design assessments and quizzes using Eduaide’s assessment builder and utilize the Feedback Bot to produce constructive, personalized feedback for learners.

  • Organize teaching tasks with Notion AI

Research Supercharged with Perplexity AI

Understanding Perplexity

This section introduces you to the concept of AI as an “Answer Engine.” It helps educators move past the limitations of traditional search engines and experience how Perplexity AI saves hours of manual searching by delivering credible, summarized, and cited information instantly.


Before watching, take a moment to reflect:

  • How do you usually search for lesson materials or data to support your teaching?

  • How confident are you in the reliability of those sources?

  • What challenges do you face in filtering credible information from the noise online?


Reflecting on these questions will help you better understand the difference between simply “searching” and truly “finding.”


Key Concepts

  • The difference between a search engine and an answer engine.

  • Why AI-powered summarized answers are more efficient.

  • How Perplexity provides cited, verifiable responses that educators can trust.


Why Educators Need to Move Beyond Google

Most teachers rely on Google to find classroom resources. However, Google is optimized for advertising and keyword ranking, not for accuracy or context.


You end up with long lists of links, requiring you to click through, read, and verify each one manually.


This method is time-intensive and often leads to inconsistent information, especially when dealing with current events or specialized topics.


What Makes Perplexity AI Different

Perplexity AI is not a search engine. It's an Answer Engine.


It reads across multiple credible sources, analyzes their content, and provides a single summarized answer with citations attached.


Think of it as a blend of Google’s reach and Wikipedia’s conciseness, but verified with sources.


Example:

Prompt: “Explain mitochondria for college students.”

Output: A detailed summary written in simple language, citing authentic sources 


How This Transforms Teaching

By cutting down the time needed to sift through irrelevant pages, Perplexity gives educators more time to design engaging learning experiences. It acts as a research assistant, delivering classroom-ready content and trustworthy references instantly.

 
 

Reflection Prompts

  • How does Perplexity’s summarized approach compare to how you currently use Google?

  • What are the potential benefits of using an “Answer Engine” in your subject area?


Mini Activity

  • Go to Perplexity.ai.

  • Ask: “Summarize Newton’s Laws of Motion in simple terms for high school students.”

  • Review the summarized explanation and check its cited sources.

  • Reflect: Did the results feel trustworthy? How long did it take compared to a Google search?


Classroom Application Example

  • Create a short reading passage or summary for your next lesson using Perplexity’s response.

  • Add the cited sources at the end as a demonstration of academic transparency for your students.

Search In Perplexity

This section introduces you to Perplexity’s Search feature, an AI-powered tool designed to transform the way educators, students, and researchers gather information.


Instead of opening multiple browser tabs or reading through countless articles, Perplexity’s Answer Engine provides summarized, verified, and visually supported responses all in one place.


Before watching, take a moment to reflect:

  • How do you currently gather visuals, explanations, and reliable sources when preparing lessons?

  • Do you often spend time checking whether your sources are credible or current?

  • How might an AI tool that summarizes, cites, and visualizes information improve your workflow?


Reflecting on these questions will help you understand why AI-driven search is not just faster, but more purposeful and evidence-based.


Key Concepts

  1. How Perplexity’s Search function differs from a traditional web search.

  2. How it gathers and displays summarized answers with sources and visuals.

  3. How the system supports follow-up queries and conversational continuity.

  4. How educators can use these results to prepare lessons, itineraries, or research summaries efficiently.


From Simple Queries to Smart Answers

Most educators start their research journey using a standard search engine, which returns a list of links that must be opened, read, and verified manually.


Perplexity simplifies this by acting as a Search + Answer Engine.


Example Prompt:

“Explain mitochondria for college students.”


Instead of returning a list of articles, Perplexity instantly provides:

  • A concise, age-appropriate explanation of mitochondria.

  • A set of related web images, automatically sourced from credible references.

  • A full list of sources it reviewed (in this case, ten verified sources).


This ensures that educators no longer need to cross-check multiple sites, the tool already consolidates and cites the information.


Contextual Continuity: From One Prompt to the Next

After generating an initial answer, Perplexity invites deeper exploration by suggesting follow-up questions.


Intelligent Follow-Ups and Related Queries

Every response in Perplexity includes a “+” icon beside related search suggestions. Clicking the icon instantly launches a new connected search, ensuring a continuous learning thread without starting over.


Exporting, Sharing, and Managing Results

Each response in Perplexity offers multiple interactive options:

  • Share Link: Generate a shareable URL for peers or students.

  • Export: Save the output as a PDF, Markdown file, or Word document.

  • Rewrite: Refine tone or complexity for different age groups.

  • Model Change: Switch to another AI model to compare outputs.

  • Copy/View Sources/Delete: Manage your responses for later use.


Remembering Context and Refining Models

Perplexity has the ability to retain context across searches. This means if you ask for transport options, accommodation details, or even export your itinerary, the system continues from the same conversation without losing prior details.


Additionally, you can switch AI models mid-session to adjust the tone, depth, or creativity of the responses, offering flexibility for academic versus administrative use.


How This Transforms Teaching and Research

By integrating summarized answers, multimedia visuals, verified sources, and conversation continuity, Perplexity’s Search:

  • Saves educators hours of research time.

  • Promotes critical evaluation of cited data.

  • Enables teachers to convert search results directly into teaching aids or class discussions.


Encourages inquiry-based learning where students can explore a topic step by step through guided follow-ups.

 
 

Reflection Prompts

  • How does Perplexity’s summarized and cited response differ from using a typical search engine like Google?

  • How might the visual and conversational features enhance your lesson preparation or student research tasks?

  • In what ways can follow-up prompts support inquiry-based learning in your classroom?


Mini Activity

  • Visit www.perplexity.ai.

  • Type: “Explain photosynthesis for high school students.”

  • Review the answer. Note the images, citations, and related questions.

  • Click one follow-up suggestion and explore how the conversation continues.

  • Reflect: How much faster and richer was this experience compared to traditional searching?


Classroom Application Example

  • Use Perplexity’s responses to create quick learning summaries for your next topic.

  • Include the sources at the end of your class notes to teach academic transparency.

  • Encourage students to ask their own follow-up questions to develop curiosity-driven research skills.

  • Export a conversation thread as a reading packet to show how a topic evolves from question to deeper understanding.


Next, you’ll move beyond simple searches into Deep Research and Pages in Perplexity.  You’ll learn how to use Perplexity’s Labs and Pages features to conduct large-scale research projects, analyze data, and compile classroom-ready reports, complete with citations, charts, and visual dashboards.

steps.title: Using Perplexity as an Answer Engine

content.htmlContent:
Using Perplexity as an Answer Engine

Deep Search & Pages in Perplexity

Let’s dive deeper into Perplexity’s capabilities. Let’s see how to craft precise, context-based prompts and understand how Perplexity processes your input to generate structured, citation-backed answers.


When you research a topic, how often do you have to simplify the information for students?


Have you ever struggled to make complex academic concepts more relatable?


Perplexity can do that simplification for you.


Key Concepts

  • How Perplexity’s Answer Engine generates contextualized summaries.

  • How to frame effective prompts for age-appropriate and curriculum-specific results.

  • How the Follow-Up Thread feature saves time by maintaining conversation history.


The Role of the Answer Engine

Perplexity uses Large Language Models (LLMs) to process user queries.

It doesn’t just “search”, it interprets context and summarizes meaningfully across several trusted databases.


Example:

Prompt: “Summarize India’s struggle for independence in 5 points for Class 10.”

Output: Five concise, historically accurate bullet points, each citing verified educational sources.


Crafting Effective Prompts

The key to successful use of Perplexity is in specific prompting.


Use the A–T–C Framework:

  • Audience: Who is this for? (e.g., “for high school students”)

  • Task: What do you want it to do? (e.g., “Summarize in bullet points”)

  • Context: What tone or example should it use? (e.g., “Include one classroom example.”)


Example Prompt:

“Explain the process of photosynthesis in 3 bullet points for a Grade 9 biology class.”


Follow-Up Threads: Context Retention

New Thread is a New chat

One of Perplexity’s most powerful features is its ability to continue conversations.


If you ask: “What is renewable energy?” 

Then follow with: “Explain the latest innovations in solar power.”


Perplexity connects both questions to build on the prior answer, enabling deeper exploration.


Educational Benefits

  • Generates simplified explanations for all subjects.

  • Provides classroom-friendly examples.

  • Ensures that every answer is properly cited.

  • Greatly reduces time spent manually rewriting or summarizing material.

 
 

Reflection Prompts

  • What type of prompt produces the best results for your subject?

  • How can you use the “Follow-Up Thread” feature to develop multi-step lesson content?


Mini Activity

  • Ask Perplexity: “Summarize global warming in 5 points for a middle school class.”

  • Then follow up: “Add a short paragraph about its impact on India.”

  • Observe how the second response connects with the first.


Classroom Application Example

  • Use Perplexity to generate both lesson summaries and student discussion questions.

  • Encourage students to build their own follow-up queries to enhance their critical thinking.
