Finding and Citing Academic Sources

Academic Focus

The Academic Focus Mode within Perplexity AI helps access verified, research-grade sources directly. Perplexity AI identifies citations, uses them ethically, and integrates them into your lesson content.


How do you currently check the credibility of the information you use in class?


Have you ever had to find academic references to support a lesson, research project, or presentation?


Why Academic Credibility Matters

Education thrives on reliability. Teachers serve as information gatekeepers.

Citing peer-reviewed or research-backed materials ensures lessons remain factually correct and defensible.


How the Academic Focus Works

  • Turn on the Academic Mode toggle in Perplexity.

  • Ask a question (e.g., “Impact of social media on learning outcomes”).

  • AI will restrict results to research articles, academic journals, and professional reports.

  • Each answer includes source metadata (author, title, publication date, and link).


Citation Integration

Each summarized response comes with clickable references.


You can copy and paste them directly into slides or lesson plans in APA or MLA format.


Ethical & Practical Use

  • Always verify sources by opening the link.

  • Avoid over-reliance on summaries. Check for context.

  • Model citation best practices for students to encourage responsible AI use.


Educational Use Cases

  • Preparing teacher training material or policy summaries.

  • Assigning students research-based projects.

  • Supporting professional development with evidence-based insights.

 
 

Reflection Prompts

  • How can citations enhance your classroom credibility?

  • How will you introduce academic referencing to your students?


Mini Activity

  1. Enable Academic Focus in Perplexity.

  2. Search for: “How does climate change affect agriculture?”

  3. Copy three cited sources.

  4. Check one manually using Google Scholar.

  5. Write a short 2-line summary of how you might use that data in your classroom.


Classroom Application Example

Use Perplexity’s academic summaries to:

  • Create annotated reading lists.

  • Support student-led research presentations.

  • Reference sources in school newsletters or departmental reports.

Finance Focus

In this section, you will learn how to use Perplexity AI’s Finance Focus to explore, analyze, and summarize information related to financial markets, economic indicators, and business data.


The Finance Focus mode enables educators, students, and researchers to access structured and credible financial insights without switching between multiple platforms or websites.


You will understand how to:

  • Navigate the Finance Focus interface.

  • Search for financial data and market updates.

  • Use Focus Mode to generate contextual, source-backed answers.

  • Apply Finance Focus for interdisciplinary learning, such as connecting finance with mathematics or economics concepts.


When researching or teaching topics related to financial markets, stock trends, or macroeconomic indicators, it can be challenging to find reliable, up-to-date data from verified sources.


Traditionally, this might require checking multiple platforms, such as news portals, stock-tracking sites, and government reports.


Perplexity’s Finance Focus simplifies this by bringing together real-time financial data, credible analysis, and government or institutional sources into a single interface.


Navigating the Finance Focus Dashboard

See the Finance Focus interface in Perplexity.


When opened, it displays:

  • A Market Summary section featuring real-time financial data.

  • A Watchlist for tracking specific stocks or funds.

  • Recent Developments offering current updates from financial news and trends.


This dashboard layout allows users to view both macro-level and micro-level insights at a glance.


Querying the Finance Focus Section

The educator demonstrates how to ask questions within the Finance Focus tab.


Example query:

“Recommend the top five stocks to invest in 2025.”


Perplexity’s Finance Focus instantly generates a detailed list of stocks, each supported by verified sources and financial data.


The key advantage here is that source transparency is built in, users can directly see references and links to the data used, such as:

  • Government or regulatory sites.

  • Financial journals or verified investment reports.


This ensures academic reliability, especially when the tool is used for teaching, presentations, or research assignments.


Analyzing Fund or Stock Performance 

The next example explores how users can analyze specific stocks or funds. 

Example Query: “How is the fund performing?”


Perplexity responds with a detailed analysis, including where the stock is listed (e.g., NASDAQ) and performance metrics, without needing to visit external finance portals such as Moneycontrol or Bloomberg.


This helps users quickly interpret investment or company data, making it useful for classroom discussions on topics like market performance, index behavior, or portfolio analysis.


Using Finance Focus for Cross-Subject Applications

The educator then creatively applies Finance Focus to a mathematics lesson, showing its interdisciplinary potential.


Even while remaining in Finance Focus, the educator enters a non-finance query: “Create a lesson plan for trigonometry including real-world applications so that students are engaged in the classroom.”


Perplexity’s AI responds with a well-structured lesson plan that includes:

  • Learning Context: Understanding sine, cosine, and tangent.

  • Practical Demonstration: Using Google Earth to explain angles of elevation and depression.

  • Real-World Applications: Examples like measuring the height of monuments or trees using trigonometric principles.

  • Collaborative Learning: Group problem-solving activities involving real-life measurements.


This part highlights that Focus Modes in Perplexity, while optimized for specific domains, are still capable of adapting prompts for educational or creative use cases.


Interactive Learning with Practice Prompts 

After generating the trigonometry lesson plan, Perplexity asks an interactive follow-up question:

“How comfortable are you with basic trigonometry concepts like sine, cosine, and tangent?”


It provides multiple-choice options ranging from “I’ve heard of them but not sure what they mean” to “I can apply trigonometry confidently in real-life scenarios.”


Based on the selected response, the system suggests practice problems and sample questions, guiding learners toward self-assessment.


This illustrates Perplexity’s potential to act as a personalized tutor within a focused context.


Using Finance Focus for Economic Research 

Next, the educator returns to a finance-related topic and queries: “Create an analysis of the GDP of India.”


Because the search is conducted within Finance Focus, Perplexity delivers:

  • A summary table with key GDP metrics.

  • A one-page executive summary highlighting trends and comparisons.

  • Source citations from credible references, particularly government websites and economic databases.


This ensures that learners and educators can rely on the results for assignments, data reports, or classroom presentations without worrying about accuracy or plagiarism.


Exploring Additional Finance Tools

The educator points out the Price Alert option available in the top-right corner of the Finance Focus interface.


Users can search for specific stocks or funds and set alerts for price changes, enabling timely tracking of financial movements.


Additionally, the Latest News section keeps users updated with current developments, integrating real-time financial updates directly into the research interface.

 
 

Key Takeaways

  • Finance Focus provides real-time financial insights, summaries, and credible source citations.

  • Users can explore markets, analyze company performance, and review macroeconomic indicators without switching platforms.

  • The tool supports cross-disciplinary learning, allowing integration of finance with mathematics, economics, and real-world problem solving.

  • Interactive follow-up prompts make the learning process more engaging and personalized.

  • Data authenticity is ensured through verified government and financial institution sources.


Now that you’ve explored Finance Focus and seen how it simplifies complex research, the next section will show you how to use the Focus Feature across different domains to gather accurate, contextual information tailored to your specific learning or teaching needs.

Using the Focus Feature

Perplexity’s Focus Modes are tools that let you filter the kind of content you want (text, video, or data). Create multimodal learning experiences by combining text-based and visual sources.


Think of a topic you often teach - for example, “Photosynthesis” or “Market Demand.” 


Ask yourself:

  • Would a visual explanation help your students more than text?

  • Would data or graphs make it clearer?


Key Concepts

  • How each Focus Mode tailors responses.

  • How to mix multiple modes for lesson variety.

  • How to ensure credibility while using diverse media.


Overview of Focus Modes

Connectors & Tasks

This section explores how Perplexity connects with external tools and automates recurring tasks through its Connectors and Tasks features. Together, these features transform Perplexity from a question-answering tool into a full-fledged digital assistant, capable of scheduling reminders, sending daily updates, summarizing articles, and interacting across multiple platforms.


Understanding Connectors

Connectors allow Perplexity to integrate with several popular applications to streamline work and learning. These include Gmail, Outlook, Google Drive, Dropbox, Notion, Linear, and GitHub. Through these integrations, Perplexity can access data, schedule reminders, manage documents, and assist with communications, all from one centralized space.


It also supports WhatsApp integration, which allows users to interact naturally with Perplexity just like a conversation with a human assistant. This makes AI assistance more accessible, not limited to the Perplexity interface, but available within daily communication channels.


Conversational Assistance via WhatsApp

When connected to WhatsApp, Perplexity can handle a variety of requests directly in chat. For example, it can:

  • Set reminders for tasks or deadlines.

  • Provide summaries of current topics or daily news.

  • Respond to general or subject-specific questions.

  • Support deep research through conversational queries.


Although certain features like clickable links may not be available within WhatsApp, the AI’s natural conversation style and quick responses make it a convenient personal assistant. This function supports teachers, students, and professionals who want immediate help without logging into the full platform, making Perplexity a versatile companion for daily task management.


The Tasks Feature

The Tasks section in Perplexity acts as an automation hub. Instead of repeating prompts every day, users can schedule recurring instructions. Each task can be customized for timing, frequency, notification method, and content source.


Common applications include:

  • Receiving daily or weekly summaries of relevant topics such as education, technology, or finance.

  • Setting study or content reminders.

  • Creating scheduled teaching tips or insights for lesson planning.

  • Monitoring price alerts or data trends.


Every scheduled task functions like a personalized AI alert, ensuring continuous updates without manual effort.


Customizing Task Settings

Each task in Perplexity offers several customization options:

  • Timing: Choose when updates should be sent; once daily, multiple times a day, or weekly.

  • Expiration date: Determine how long the task should remain active.

  • Notification channel: Select delivery through in-app notifications, email, or mobile alerts.

  • AI model selection: Use the default model or choose from options like Claude 4.5 Sonar for specialized performance.

  • Source preference: Choose whether the information should come from web sources or academic databases, depending on purpose.


Once saved, Perplexity begins delivering consistent updates based on these settings.


Automating Professional and Educational Routines

Perplexity’s Tasks can be adapted for both professional and classroom use. For example:

  • Teachers can receive daily lesson-planning tips or reminders for synchronous and asynchronous classes.

  • Researchers can schedule weekly research summaries or topic updates.

  • Students can set study reminders or daily motivational notes.

  • Professionals can schedule market updates or industry news reports relevant to their field.


This automation helps maintain continuous learning and productivity while reducing time spent on repetitive information searches.


Notifications and Output Management

Once tasks are active, Perplexity automatically sends notifications based on the chosen delivery mode. Email notifications include a summary of the update (e.g., “Top Tech Developments of the Week”) and a link to view the complete, source-cited response in Perplexity.


This method replaces the need for multiple website subscriptions or newsletters, providing consolidated, verified information in one place. All task outputs can also be saved directly into Spaces for future reference or organized as Pages for easy sharing with colleagues or students.


Practical Value

Connectors and Tasks together represent a shift from reactive to proactive use of AI. Rather than waiting for a user query, Perplexity anticipates needs, retrieves credible information, and delivers it at scheduled times. This makes it particularly effective for:

  • Educators who want AI support for lesson updates or planning prompts.

  • Students who prefer structured, automated learning reminders.

  • Professionals who track sector developments without manual searching.

 
 

Key Takeaways

  • Connectors enable Perplexity to link with external apps such as Gmail, Drive, Notion, and WhatsApp.

  • You can chat naturally with Perplexity through integrated platforms like WhatsApp.

  • Tasks automate research, updates, and reminders , similar to scheduled alerts.

  • Tasks can deliver results daily, weekly, or at specific times, with your preferred model and source settings.

  • Notifications can be sent via email, mobile, or in-app, depending on your preference.

  • This feature turns Perplexity into a personal AI assistant that manages your academic and professional information flow.


Now that you’ve learned how Connectors and Tasks help automate workflows and information delivery, the next section introduces Comet, a feature that takes Perplexity’s capabilities further by enhancing collaborative research, file handling, and advanced project management within shared spaces.

Comet

This section introduces Comet, Perplexity’s own AI-powered browser. Comet looks and functions much like a traditional web browser, but it integrates directly with Perplexity’s intelligence to perform multi-step actions, open resources automatically, and summarize information, all without manual effort.


What is Comet?

Comet is an AI-integrated browser built within Perplexity. It behaves like Chrome or Edge but includes a built-in assistant that automates browsing tasks. Instead of opening each tab manually, searching for sites, or managing multiple tools separately, Comet can complete these actions with a single instruction.


This browser is designed for efficiency. It supports educators, students, and professionals by combining research, content creation, and media exploration in one place.


Why Use Comet?

Comet is especially useful for:

  • Creating workspaces instantly (for presentations, research, or design).

  • Accessing and controlling media content like YouTube videos.

  • Summarizing academic articles or PDFs into quick, readable formats.


It eliminates repetitive steps such as opening, arranging, and switching between multiple sites, saving time and streamlining digital tasks.


Example 1: Creating a Quick Pitch Workspace

Comet can generate an entire workspace setup with a single command. When asked to “spin up a quick pitch workspace” and open:

  • Google Slides

  • A royalty-free image site

  • A color palette tool


Comet automatically opens all three tabs, groups them under “Pitch Deck,” and prepares them for immediate use. This means users no longer have to manually search for each site, open separate windows, or set up their creative resources individually. Comet performs these steps instantly, creating an organized digital workspace in seconds, ideal for teachers designing presentations or students preparing projects.


Example 2: Watching a YouTube Video

Comet is used to locate and play a specific YouTube video, the historic Apollo 11 moon landing featuring Neil Armstrong.


When prompted with the command to open the video, Comet directly retrieves it and starts playback. It can even respond to follow-up instructions such as, “Show me the timestamp where he lands.”


Using its AI capabilities, Comet analyzes the video’s subtitles and pinpoints the exact timestamp (1 minute 11 seconds) when Armstrong steps onto the moon and says, “That’s one small step for a man, one giant leap for mankind.”


This shows how Comet merges search accuracy with interactive learning, helping users find precise moments or details within videos,  a valuable feature for classroom demonstrations or lesson preparation.


Example 3: Researching an Academic Article

When asked to open a Harvard web research article on Venture Capital, Comet instantly retrieves credible academic sources, including PDFs.


Upon selecting one article, the user can request: “Explain this in a one-page bullet-point summary.”


Comet reads through the lengthy text (for example, an 80-page paper) and provides a concise, one-page summary that captures the key findings and arguments.


This showcases Comet’s potential for research and study, transforming long, time-consuming readings into quick, structured insights that support faster understanding and lesson planning.


Key Features to Note

  • AI-Driven Navigation: Comet performs web tasks automatically from a single prompt.

  • Workspace Creation: Groups relevant tools or sites into organized tabs for focused work.

  • Intelligent Media Interaction: Finds and highlights precise moments in videos.

  • Academic Support: Summarizes lengthy or complex research papers into simple summaries.


These features make Comet both a time-saving productivity tool and a learning aid for educators and students alike.

 
 

Key Takeaways

  • Comet is an AI-powered browser integrated within Perplexity that automates browsing and research.

  • It works like Chrome but responds to natural-language instructions.

  • It can create entire digital workspaces, such as for a “pitch deck,” by opening and grouping relevant tabs.

  • It identifies specific video moments, such as timestamps in YouTube videos, by analyzing subtitles.

  • It provides bullet-point summaries of lengthy academic articles, saving hours of manual reading.

  • Overall, Comet demonstrates how AI can transform everyday browsing into an intelligent, guided, and time-efficient experience.


Now that you’ve explored how Comet automates browsing, workspace setup, and content summarization, the next section introduces Lesson Planning with Eduaide.AI, a platform that helps educators design structured, engaging lesson plans with AI support.

Introduction to Eduaide

Let’s understand what Eduaide.AI is, why it was designed for educators, and what core features you will use to plan lessons faster and more consistently.


Think about your last lesson plan:

  • What part took the most time?

  • What would you change if you had an assistant that could draft that lesson in minutes?


Key Concepts:

  • How Eduaide organizes a lesson plan (sections and terminology).

  • The main dashboard elements: prompt area, templates, export options.

  • Privacy, export formats, and how outputs map to classroom documents.


What is Eduaide.AI and why it matters

Eduaide.AI is an AI tool built specifically to help teachers create lesson plans, activities, and assessments quickly. It is tuned to the curriculum and classroom workflow so that outputs are usable with minimal editing. For teachers this means less time drafting and more time on pedagogy and student interactions.


Core dashboard and workflow

The main Eduaide workflow has a few consistent parts: a prompt box where you type grade, topic, time, and objective; a templates area with common lesson structures (direct instruction, inquiry, project-based); a differentiation toggle; assessment generator; and a feedback or refine button. The system is designed so a teacher can move from idea to first draft in one or two clicks.


Input types and specificity

Eduaide accepts simple prompts (e.g., “Fractions for Grade 4, 45 minutes, objective: add and subtract fractions with like denominators”) and it also accepts more complex inputs (standards codes, materials available, digital tools). The more specific your input, the more tailored the lesson will be.


Output formats and export

Generated lessons can be exported as PDF, Word, Google Docs, or LMS-ready HTML. The output includes objectives, warm-up, guided practice, independent practice, assessment, materials list, and time breakdowns. Teachers can quickly copy-paste into their LMS or handouts.


Privacy and data handling

Eduaide may store prompts or outputs depending on account settings. Always check institutional policy before pasting sensitive student data. Use anonymized examples when testing.

 
 

Mini Tasks

  1. Log into Eduaide.ai or your institution’s account.

  2. Explore the dashboard; find the main prompt box, templates section, and export options.

  3. Generate a short plan using the prompt: “Fractions for Grade 4 (45 mins).”

  4. Observe how quickly a structured lesson appears and save your result.


Reflection & Classroom Application

  • What features stood out as most practical for your classroom needs?

  • Did the structure of Eduaide’s lesson plan match your preferred planning format?

  • How might this tool help you balance administrative tasks with creative teaching?


Now that you’ve seen what Eduaide can do at a glance, it’s time to go deeper. In the next video, we’ll explore how to generate complete, end-to-end lesson plans using simple prompts,  and then refine them to meet your exact classroom context.


You’ll see how a short, one-line idea like “Fractions for Grade 4” can become a fully detailed, standards-aligned lesson, complete with objectives, warm-ups, guided practice, assessments, and timing breakdowns.

Generating Complete Lesson Plans

Lesson Plan: Convert a simple idea into a full lesson plan using Eduaide’s prompt-and-generate features, and refine generated plans for classroom readiness.


Key Concepts:

  • How prompts are structured for complete plans (grade, duration, objectives, materials).

  • The typical components returned in a lesson plan.

  • How to request alignment with standards or learning outcomes.


Anatomy of a generated lesson plan

Eduaide typically returns lesson components in a consistent format: Title, Standards/Outcomes, Objectives (specific, measurable), Materials, Time breakdown, Intro / Hook, Direct Instruction steps, Guided Practice activities, Independent Practice tasks, Assessment items, Differentiation notes, and Homework or extension tasks.


Prompt engineering for full plans

Use structured prompts. Include: (a) grade, (b) topic, (c) length, (d) desired standard (if any), (e) preferred activities (e.g., “use manipulatives” or “group-based debate”), (f) assessment type. Example prompt for a college class: “90-minute session on ethical frameworks in business, include 15-min case discussion and 30-min group activity”.


Customizing and refining outputs

After generation, teachers will edit for local context: change examples to local case studies, adapt timings, and add links to school resources. Eduaide often supports a refine button where you can ask the AI to make the plan shorter, more interactive, or to add an assessment.


Examples

  • Elementary example: Fractions Grade 4, 45 mins: includes manipulatives, number line demo, 15-min guided practice, exit ticket.

  • College example: Business Ethics 90 mins:  includes scenario analysis, small-group role play, rubric for assessment.

 
 

Mini Tasks

  1. Choose a topic you’ll actually teach soon.

  2. Enter a structured prompt including grade, duration, and learning goals.

  3. Generate your complete lesson plan and check for clarity and sequence.

  4. Export and save it in your preferred format (Word, PDF, or Google Docs).


Reflection & Classroom Application

  • How close was the generated plan to what you’d normally create?

  • What sections (objectives, timing, or activities) needed the most editing?

  • How could you reuse this feature weekly to save prep time?


You’ve seen how Eduaide instantly builds complete, structured lesson plans, but every classroom is unique.


In the upcoming video, you’ll explore how Eduaide adapts those lessons for different learning levels and needs. Eduaide can automatically create beginner, intermediate, and advanced versions of the same lesson, ensuring every student learns at their own pace.

Creating Differentiated Content

Let’s understand Eduaide’s differentiation features and how to produce beginner, intermediate, and advanced materials quickly to meet diverse learner needs.


Think of three learners in your class: one who struggles, one at-level, one who needs enrichment. What support would each need for the same lesson?


Key Concepts:

  • How to request differentiated outputs (reading level, scaffolds, extension tasks).

  • Examples of scaffolded supports, sentence frames, visuals and challenge tasks.


Models of differentiation the tool supports

Eduaide supports multiple differentiation approaches: leveled content (simple → complex), scaffolding (more prompts and supports for beginners), compacting (enrichment tasks for advanced learners), and choice boards.


Prompting for differentiation

Add qualifiers to your prompt such as “provide three versions: beginner (simple language, guided steps), intermediate (classroom activities), advanced (extension project and critical thinking question).” The tool will return side-by-side variants.


Differentiation outputs and artifacts

Outputs may include: simplified text, guided worksheets with sentence starters, visual aids suggestions, extension projects, rubrics for varied performance levels, and accommodation notes for ELLs or learners with IEPs.


Examples and classroom use

  • Example for English class on metaphor:

    • Beginner: identify metaphors with examples

    • Intermediate: write two metaphors

    • Advanced: craft a short story using five metaphors and peer-review rubric.


  • Example for science class:

    • Beginner: worksheet with labeled diagrams

    • Advanced: lab-based design challenge.


Universal Design for Learning (UDL) alignment

Eduaide’s differentiated outputs can be used to provide multiple means of representation (text, visuals), action and expression (written, oral), and engagement (choices, challenges).

 
 

Mini Tasks

  1. Open one of your previously generated lesson plans.

  2. Ask Eduaide to create three differentiated versions; one each for beginner, intermediate, and advanced learners.

  3. Compare the structure and tone of each version.

  4. Adjust and save the one you’d use for your current class.


Reflection & Classroom Application

  • Which differentiation strategy (simplified text, scaffolded activity, enrichment task) felt most practical for your students?

  • How could this feature help in mixed-ability or inclusive classrooms?

  • What other ways could AI support you in personalizing instruction?


Now that your lessons are differentiated and tailored for diverse learners, it’s time to think about how you’ll evaluate their understanding.


In the next video, Eduaide helps you move from planning to measuring, by generating quizzes, project prompts, and rubrics that align perfectly with your learning objectives.

  • What other ways could AI support you in personalizing instruction?


Now that your lessons are differentiated and tailored for diverse learners, it’s time to think about how you’ll evaluate their understanding.


In the next video, Eduaide helps you move from planning to measuring, by generating quizzes, project prompts, and rubrics that align perfectly with your learning objectives.

Using the Feedback Bot

The Feedback Bot is used to evaluate and improve lesson drafts, worksheets, and assessments for clarity, engagement, inclusivity, and alignment.


When someone reviews your lesson plan, what feedback would be most helpful: clarity, pacing, differentiation, or assessment alignment?


What the Feedback Bot evaluates

The Feedback Bot runs a checklist over your document. If you want a feedback on lesson plan ..It assesses clarity of learning objectives, alignment between activities and assessment, engagement strategies, differentiation, estimated timings, and inclusivity (language, cultural sensitivity, accessibility). If you want to provide a feedback on student essays it will.


Common feedback categories and examples

  • Clarity: “Objective reads as ‘understand’. Consider making it measurable: ‘Describe three effects of X.’”

  • Engagement: “Add an interactive entry activity to hook students for 5 minutes.”

  • Differentiation: “Include a scaffolded version or sentence frames for ELL students.”

  • Assessment alignment: “Add a formative check after guided practice to inform instruction.”

  • Accessibility: “Provide alt-text for images and a transcript for any video.”


How to iterate

You accept suggestions, or ask the bot to propose alternatives. The bot can regenerate sections (e.g., rewrite the hook) and provide multiple options. It keeps a version history so you can compare drafts.


Using Feedback Bot/Erasmus for quality assurance

Use the bot as a peer reviewer. It is especially useful when you work alone and need a second pair of eyes. 


Limitations and teacher control

The Feedback Bot suggests improvements but cannot replace professional judgement. Teachers must vet suggestions for curricular fit and cultural appropriateness.


Mini Tasks

  1. Upload one of your lesson plans or student submissions into the Feedback Bot.

  2. Review the AI’s comments 

  3. Accept or modify at least three suggested improvements.

  4. Save both the “before” and “after” versions to compare quality.


Reflection & Classroom Application

  • Which feedback areas were most useful?

  • Did the bot highlight blind spots that you hadn’t considered?

  • How might this tool complement peer feedback in your department?

M2 - Task 2

Generate a complete lesson plan for one class, including a differentiated content piece and a 5-question assessment.

(Estimated time: 120 mins)


Objective: The goal of this task is to use Eduaide.ai to generate a full lesson plan for one class session. This includes the lesson’s objective, teaching strategies, differentiated content for diverse learners, and a short assessment to check understanding. You will experience how AI can streamline lesson planning while still allowing room for creativity and teacher judgment.


Instructions:

Choose Your Lesson Topic: Select a topic from your subject area that fits within a single class period (approximately 45-60 minutes). It can be from any subject or skill area, such as:


  • The Water Cycle (Science)

  • The Pythagorean Theorem (Mathematics)

  • Figurative Language (English)

  • Human Rights and Responsibilities (Social Studies)

  • The Importance of Empathy (Value Education / SEL)


Use Eduaide.ai to Generate Your Lesson Plan: Open Eduaide.ai and follow these steps:

Choose “Lesson Plan Generator” from the tools menu. Enter your chosen topic along with grade level, learning objective, and preferred teaching style (e.g., activity-based, inquiry-driven, or direct instruction).


Ask Eduaide to include:

  • Lesson Objective(s)

  • Materials Required

  • Step-by-step Teaching Procedure

  • Differentiated Content for varying learner levels (e.g., visual aids, simplified texts, or extension tasks)

  • A 5-question assessment to test understanding (mix of multiple-choice, short answer, or reflection questions).


Example Prompt: “Create a 45-minute lesson plan for Grade 7 students on the topic ‘Photosynthesis.’ Include clear objectives, a warm-up activity, a hands-on experiment, differentiated content for mixed-ability learners, and a 5-question quiz at the end.”


Review and Refine Your Lesson Plan: 

Once Eduaide generates your lesson plan: 

  • Read through each section carefully.

  • Edit or adapt the activities to better match your teaching context or student needs.

  • Add or modify differentiation strategies to support advanced, struggling, and special needs learners.

  • Review the 5-question assessment to ensure it aligns with the lesson objectives.


Add Your Personal Touch: Enhance your AI-generated plan by adding:

  • Real-world examples or connections to students’ experiences.

  • Interactive elements such as games, short videos, or group discussions.

  • Visual aids or digital tools that make learning more engaging.


Reflect on the Experience:

After finalizing your lesson plan, take a moment to consider:

  • How effectively did Eduaide structure your lesson?

  • Did it save time in planning while maintaining quality?

  • How well did the AI incorporate differentiation and assessment elements?


Checklist & Self-Reflection:

  1. Selected a clear, single-class topic.

  2. Generated a complete lesson plan using Eduaide’s Lesson Plan Generator.

  3. Included differentiated content for diverse learners.

  4. Added a 5-question assessment aligned with objectives.

  5. Reviewed and refined the plan for accuracy and engagement.

  6. Reflected on how AI supported your teaching design process.


Self-Reflection Question: How did Eduaide help you address the diverse learning needs of students while saving time in lesson planning?


Once your lesson plan and reflection are complete, mark this activity as ‘Complete’ in the LMS.

steps.title: The Organized Teacher with Notion AI

content.htmlContent:
The Organized Teacher with Notion AI

Notion as a Digital Workspace

Let’s Understand the role of Notion as a single workspace for teaching: store research, build lesson plans, manage projects, and create living documents that you and your team can update and search.


  1. Where do you currently keep lesson drafts, research links, meeting notes, and to-do lists?

  2. Which part of your workflow causes the most friction - finding an old file, remembering a link, or sharing materials with colleagues?


Key Concepts:

  • How a Notion workspace is structured (workspace → pages → subpages → databases).

  • Basic building blocks: text blocks, headings, toggles, callouts, embeds.

  • How Notion AI augments writing, summarizing, and brainstorming inside pages.


Notion’s Information Hierarchy and Why It Matters

Notion organizes content as pages that can contain any content type and can be nested. Pages can become rich “documents” (lesson plans, research dossiers) or function as databases (trackers, gradebooks). A consistent hierarchy, or “My Teaching Hub” with subpages for Research, Lesson Plans, Meetings, and Projects, keeps everything discoverable.


Example structure:

  • My Teaching Hub (top page)

    • Research (subpage)

    • Lesson Plans (database)

    • Reflections (journal)

    • Meetings (database)

    • Projects (database)


This structure avoids file duplication and lets you search across all content.


Blocks: The Building Blocks of Notion

Notion content is made of blocks: paragraph, heading, bullet list, toggle, code, image, embed, table, etc. Understanding blocks allows you to quickly reformat text, collapse sections, or turn bullets into checklists.


Example use: Put a complicated policy into a toggle list to hide details during a presentation and expand for staff reading.


Databases vs. Pages

Databases are collections of items with properties (columns). Each row is a page. Databases can show different views: table, gallery, list, kanban (board), calendar, and timeline. Use databases for repeatable, trackable items (lesson plans, projects, meeting notes).


Example: A Lesson Plans database with properties: Course, Topic, Grade, Status, Owner, Start Date, Duration, Resources (files/links), Tags (standards), and AI Summary.



Notion AI: Where it Helps

Notion AI can:

  • Generate first drafts from prompts.

  • Summarize long notes into bullets or an executive summary.

  • Convert raw meeting notes into an agenda or action items.

  • Draft project outlines and timelines.


Sample Notion AI prompt:

“Summarize this 1,200-word article into five classroom-friendly bullet points and suggest a 10-minute in-class activity.”


Collaboration & Sharing

Notion supports team workspaces, permissions (view, comment, edit), and real-time collaboration. You can mention teammates, assign tasks, and comment on pages. Use permissions to control who edits curriculum materials.


Integrations & Embeds

Notion can embed Google Docs, Sheets, Slides, YouTube, and external links. Embedding Perplexity outputs (copied text or linked source list) centralizes research and lesson resources.



Best Practices: Naming, Templates, and Search

  • Use clear naming: YYYY-MM-DD_Topic or Course_Topic_Version.

  • Build templates for lesson plans, meeting notes, and project briefs to ensure consistency.

  • Tag pages with subject and semester to speed search.

  • Regularly archive outdated pages to avoid clutter.


Practical Setup Tasks

  1. Create your main Notion page titled “My Teaching Hub.”

  2. Add subpages like Research, Lesson Plans, Reflections, Meetings, and Projects.

  3. Add one sample entry in each page to start testing how information flows.

  4. Experiment with a few block types, such as toggles, callouts, and embedded links—to make your layout clean and interactive.


Reflection & Classroom Application

  • Does your workspace reflect how you actually teach and plan?

  • How might centralizing all your notes and plans in one place save time during the week?

  • What existing tools (Google Docs, Drive folders, sticky notes) could you now replace with Notion?


Now that you’ve built the foundation of your digital teaching workspace, it’s time to start putting that space to work.


In the next section, we’ll explore how Notion AI helps you turn messy meeting notes into structured, professional agendas, complete with action items, owners, and follow-up reminders.


This feature is especially powerful for teachers and administrators who handle weekly department meetings or project discussions. You’ll see how to move from scattered notes to organized, shareable pages in seconds.


So, as you move into the next section, imagine how every future meeting could start and end with clarity and purpose.

Meeting Agendas from Notes

Learn to turn unstructured meeting notes into a clear, timed agenda with action items and owners using Notion AI. This saves time and improves meeting outcomes.


Think of your last staff meeting: were the notes clear, and did they produce follow-ups?


How could a structured agenda and automated action items improve the next meeting?


Key Concepts:

  • How to paste raw notes into Notion and use Notion AI to generate an agenda.

  • How AI extracts action items, decisions, and follow-ups.

  • How to assign tasks and set reminders inside the meeting page.


From Raw Notes to a Structured Agenda

In practice, meetings produce long text: comments, ideas, and decisions. Notion AI can analyze this text and produce a structured agenda with: meeting purpose, time allocation per item, presenter, desired outcome, and action items.


Step-by-step:

  1. Create a Meeting page or open your Meetings database.

  2. Paste raw notes (bullets or transcript).

  3. Invoke Notion AI command: “Create agenda from notes for a 30-minute staff meeting.”

  4. Notion AI outputs an agenda with time slots, items, person responsible, and expected decisions.



Extracting Action Items & Assigning Owners

Notion AI can highlight action items automatically. You can convert action lines into database entries or tasks and use the @mention to assign owners. These tasks appear in each person’s workspace.


Example action: “Follow up with lab manager about equipment.” Add property: Due Date, Owner, Status.


Creating Meeting Templates

Save agenda and action item structures as a template. Use this template for weekly department meetings to maintain consistency.


Benefits for Teacher Teams

  • Meetings become outcome-focused.

  • Follow-ups are tracked and visible.

  • New staff can catch up quickly by reading agendas and action items.


Example Prompt for Notion AI

“From these notes, create a 45-minute agenda with a 10-minute status round, 20-minute curriculum discussion, and 15-minute action planning. List three action items with owners.”


Practical Tasks

  1. Create a Meetings database in your Notion workspace.

  2. Paste raw meeting notes from a recent school or team meeting.

  3. Use Notion AI to generate a 30-minute agenda that includes discussion points, time allocations, and assigned action items.

  4. Tag each action item with an owner using @mentions, and add due dates for accountability.


Reflection & Classroom Application

  • How did AI handle incomplete or unclear notes? Did it capture the key themes accurately?

  • Did structuring the agenda make it easier to share meeting outcomes with colleagues?

  • How could this process help your teaching team stay aligned between meetings?


You’ve just learned how to convert unstructured notes into a clear, actionable agenda, turning every meeting into a productive collaboration.


Now, let’s extend that organization to something even bigger: planning and managing classroom or school projects.


In the next section, you’ll see how Notion AI can help you plan multi-week events such as science fairs, art exhibitions, or community projects.


You’ll learn to generate complete project outlines with timelines, milestones, responsibilities, and deliverables, all neatly organized and trackable within your digital workspace.


Think of it as transforming your classroom into a mini organization, where students, teachers, and timelines come together with ease.

Generating Project Outlines

Learn to use Notion AI to produce structured project outlines, timelines, milestones, and responsibilities, useful for class projects, science fairs, or departmental initiatives.


Think of an upcoming project (e.g., science fair, symposium). Which parts are hardest to plan: timeline, task allocation, or milestones?


Key Concepts:

  • How Notion AI converts a project brief into a timeline and milestone list.

  • How to convert milestones into database tasks and link resources.

  • How AI suggests realistic timelines and deliverables.


Defining a Project in Notion

A project page contains summary, objectives, scope, stakeholders, milestones, tasks, timeline, and resources. Notion allows you to create a Project database where each project has linked tasks and status properties.



Example project components:

  • Title: Science Fair 2025

  • Objective: Showcase student applied research on renewable energy.

  • Scope: Grades 9–12, poster + demo + report.

  • Stakeholders: Teachers, lab manager, parent volunteers.


Generating an Outline with Notion AI

Input a short brief and ask Notion AI: “Create a 6-step project plan with milestones, deliverables, approximate time estimates, and roles.” The AI will output a sequence of milestones (proposal, research, prototype, testing, final prep, showcase) and approximate durations.


Turning Milestones into Tasks & Timeline Views

Convert milestones into a database view (timeline/calendar). Assign each milestone to an owner and add dependencies (you can note dependencies as a property). Use the timeline view to visualize overlap and resource conflicts.


How to do it:

  1. Create a Projects database and a Tasks database.

  2. Use relation property to link Tasks to Projects.

  3. Create a timeline view filtered to the Project.



Risk & Resource Management Suggestions from AI

Notion AI can suggest risks (e.g., equipment delays) and mitigation (reserve lab time in advance). It can suggest required resources: space, supervision, and budgets.


Example Prompt

“Plan a science fair project timeline for a 10-week period. Include milestones, student deliverables, teacher check-ins, and a rubric for judging.”


Practical Tasks

  1. Create a new Projects database in your Notion workspace.

  2. Add one upcoming event or project (e.g., “Annual Science Fair 2025” or “Cultural Week”).

  3. Ask Notion AI to generate a project plan with 6–8 milestones, including roles, deadlines, and deliverables.

  4. Switch to a Timeline View to visualize when each stage happens.

  5. Assign team members or students to specific tasks.


Reflection & Classroom Application

  • How did Notion AI help break down your project into manageable steps?

  • Which milestones seemed too broad or too detailed—how did you adjust them?

  • Can you imagine using this workflow with students for project-based learning or group assignments?


You’ve now built a dynamic project plan, complete with timelines and task assignments. But what happens when you need to process large amounts of research, readings, or reports related to that project?


In real classrooms, educators often face the challenge of condensing lengthy materials into digestible content for students.


That’s exactly what we will explore next. We’ll use Notion AI’s summarization feature to simplify complex documents into clear, accessible overviews.


We shall generate summaries, discussion prompts, or quiz questions from long articles, helping both you and your students focus on key takeaways rather than getting lost in pages of text.

Summarizing Long Text Blocks

Learn to use Notion AI to compress long articles, policy documents, or research reports into short, classroom-ready summaries, bullets, or discussion prompts.


Think of a long reading you’d like to use in class. What parts would you ask students to read, and which parts would you summarize for them?


Key Concepts:

  • How to paste long text and issue effective summarization prompts.

  • How to ask Notion AI for different summary styles: TL;DR, bullets, teachable points, quiz questions.


Why Summarization Matters

Long articles and reports are valuable but time-consuming. Summaries enable teachers to present core ideas, create pre-reading packets, and scaffold student reading.



Types of Summaries Notion AI Can Generate

  • Short bullets (5 points): Quick takeaways for lesson starters.

  • TL;DR (one paragraph): A single-paragraph gist for busy students.

  • Student-friendly summary: Rewrites complex language for lower reading levels.

  • Discussion prompts / quiz questions: Turn content into classroom tasks.

  • Annotated summary: Summaries with inline citations or resource links.


How to Prompt Notion AI Effectively

Provide instructions on tone and audience. For example:

“Summarize the following 2,000-word article in five bullets for undergraduate students. Include one suggested 10-minute classroom activity.”


Using Summaries in Class

Summaries can become slide content, prompts for group work, or the basis for formative assessment. You can attach the full article below the summary for students who want to dig deeper.


Handling Sensitive or Complex Content

Always verify AI summaries for nuance and accuracy, especially for controversial or technical topics. Use the original sources for verification.


Practical Tasks

  1. Paste a long text (such as a research article, chapter excerpt, or policy document) into a new Notion page.

  2. Ask Notion AI: “Summarize this text into five bullet points and two short discussion questions for undergraduate students.”

  3. Review the summary for accuracy and tone.

  4. Save your summarized notes in the Research section of your workspace.

  5. Optionally, create a “TL;DR” section for every research article you collect moving forward.


Reflection & Classroom Application

  • Did the summary accurately capture the main ideas and key evidence?

  • How could summarized materials help your students focus on comprehension rather than volume?

  • In what ways might summarization support accessibility for multilingual or differently-abled learners?


You’ve now mastered how to distill large chunks of information into short, teachable summaries.


But research and planning are only as powerful as your ability to organize and track them efficiently.


In the next section, we shall use Notion’s database and table features to track your lessons, projects, student progress, and even professional reflections.


We shall create a Lesson Implementation Tracker, add key properties like objectives and due dates, and switch between calendar or Kanban views to visualize your teaching workflow.

M2 - Task 3

Create a Notion page to organize the research from Task 1 and the lesson plan from Task 2, using AI to generate a summary and a project outline.

(Estimated time: 90 mins)


Objective: Now that you’ve completed your research in Perplexity (Task 1) and created a full lesson plan using Eduaide (Task 2), it’s time to bring everything together in one organized space. The goal of this task is to use Notion to create a professional, well-structured workspace that displays your research findings, lesson plan, and AI-generated summaries. This will help you experience how digital organization tools can streamline teaching preparation and project management.


Instructions:

Create Your Workspace in Notion: Open Notion and either create a new workspace or add a new page to an existing one. Title your page something like “AI Teaching Project: Research and Lesson Design.” Your Notion page should include the following main sections:

  • Section 1: Research Summary (from Perplexity)

  • Section 2: Lesson Plan (from Eduaide)

  • Section 3: AI-Generated Summary and Project Outline


Add and Organize Your Content: Copy your research notes and sources from Task 1 into the “Research Summary” section. Paste your lesson plan from Task 2 under the “Lesson Plan” section. Use headings, bullet points, and toggle lists to keep the content clean and easy to navigate. Add dividers, callout boxes, or emojis to make the layout visually engaging.


Tip: You can use Notion templates such as “Lesson Planner,” “Project Tracker,” or “Research Database” to make your workspace more interactive and professional.


Use AI in Notion to Summarize and Outline: In each section, highlight your content and use Notion’s built-in AI features to:

  • Generate a concise summary of your Perplexity research.

  • Create a project outline showing how your research connects to your lesson plan.

  • Suggest improvements or next steps for classroom application.


Example Prompt for Notion AI:

“Summarize this research in 100 words and create a short project outline linking this topic to the classroom lesson plan below.”


Customize and Enhance Your Workspace: Add icons or cover images that match your topic (e.g., books for English, globe for Social Studies). Link relevant files, images, or external resources for easy access. If you want, embed your presentation from Task 1 or 2 to create a complete digital portfolio view.


Reflect on the Process: Once your workspace is complete, review how all elements come together. Consider:

  • Is the organization logical and visually appealing?

  • Does the AI-generated summary accurately capture your ideas?

  • How can this workspace serve as a portfolio or planning tool for future lessons?


Checklist & Self-Reflection:

  • Created a Notion workspace with three clear sections.

  • Added research notes from Perplexity (Task 1).

  • Added the Eduaide lesson plan (Task 2).

  • Used Notion AI to generate a summary and project outline.

  • Enhanced the workspace layout for clarity and visual appeal.

  • Reflected on how Notion helped organize your AI-assisted teaching materials.


Self-Reflection Question: How did combining your research and lesson plan in Notion help you see connections between information gathering, lesson design, and digital organization?


Once your Notion workspace is organized and reviewed, mark this activity as ‘Complete’ in the LMS.

title: M3: AI in Presentation & Visual Design

content.htmlContent:
M3 - Learning Objectives

By the end of this module, educators will be able to:

  • Generate instant, visually appealing presentations using Gamma

  • Apply effective prompting strategies to produce accurate and creative AI-generated images.

  • Create AI-assisted web pages and documents in Gamma that effectively communicate learning content for students and parents.

  • Use Canva’s Magic Write to produce clear and structured classroom content such as lesson outlines, announcements, worksheets, and newsletters.

  • Adapt and fine-tune AI-generated content, ensuring appropriateness for age, curriculum, and accessibility needs.

  • Organize and export final teaching materials (presentations, handouts, web pages) into formats suitable for instruction, sharing, or assessment.

Instant Presentations with Gamma

From Prompt to Presentation

We all know how time-consuming it can be to make classroom presentations. Between content planning, slide design, and formatting, a simple 10-slide deck can easily take hours. But with AI, those long hours can shrink to minutes.


In this section, we’ll introduce Gamma, an AI-powered presentation generator that can transform your ideas into full, beautifully structured slides in less than a minute.


You’ll see how Gamma understands your prompt, organizes your ideas into a logical flow, adds visuals, and even chooses layouts automatically. You’ll learn how to go from a single line of text to a ready-to-present deck instantly.


What is Gamma?

Gamma is an AI presentation creation tool that blends the intelligence of ChatGPT with the design flexibility of tools like PowerPoint and Canva. It’s not just a slide maker. It’s a content-thinking assistant.


When you give it a topic, Gamma:

  • Interprets your idea (semantic understanding)

  • Generates a structure (logical slide sequence)

  • Designs layouts and visuals automatically


Example: Typing “Create a presentation on how space exploration inspires classroom learning” instantly produces a full 8-slide presentation.


How Gamma Works: From Prompt to Slides


When you type a prompt, Gamma follows an internal workflow:

  • Step 1: Understanding Context – Who the presentation is for (students, parents, colleagues)

  • Step 2: Structuring Content – Creating an intro, main sections, and conclusion

  • Step 3: Visual Mapping – Adding visuals or icons relevant to your topic

  • Step 4: Layout Design – Applying a clean, consistent format


All of this happens in a few seconds. A built-in AI model writes and designs simultaneously, so there’s no need to switch between writing and designing tools.


The Power of Prompts

The quality of your output depends entirely on the clarity of your input. A vague prompt like “Space exploration” produces generic slides.


A detailed prompt like “Create an 8-slide presentation for middle school students on how space exploration inspires creativity in science learning” results in relevant, engaging slides tailored for your classroom.


Prompt Tip: Be specific about:

  • Audience: Who it’s for

  • Purpose: Why you’re making it

  • Tone: How you want it to sound

  • Format: Number of slides or slide type


Benefits for Educators

  • Saves 2–3 hours of slide creation time per lesson

  • Maintains consistent visual design

  • Provides a strong draft that you can personalize

  • Encourages storytelling-based learning

 
 

You’ve just seen how quickly Gamma can turn an idea into a complete presentation. But AI isn’t meant to replace your creativity. It’s meant to accelerate it.

Generating, Editing, and Refining Presentations

Creating a presentation instantly is amazing, but no AI tool will get it perfect on the first try. That’s where your role as an educator comes in. With Gamma you can fine-tune your slides.


  • Edit and rewrite content directly within Gamma

  • Expand or shorten sections intelligently

  • Adjust tone and complexity for your audience

  • Collaborate with colleagues in real time


You can transform an auto-generated presentation into a polished, personalized teaching resource.


Editing Text and Slides

Gamma allows you to click directly on any text box and modify it. Use AI rewrite commands like:

  • “Simplify this” (makes it easier for students)

  • “Make this sound more professional”

  • “Add a classroom example”


This helps adapt the AI-generated slides to your unique teaching voice.


Expanding or Summarizing Content

Gamma supports instruction-based adjustments. You can type commands such as:

  • “Add a slide about student projects on Mars exploration.”

  • “Summarize this section in 2 slides.”


Gamma will intelligently reorganize content while keeping the narrative flow intact.


Adjusting Tone

Tone personalization is powerful in education. You can choose from tones like:

  • Formal: For academic presentations or workshops

  • Friendly: For classroom use

  • Persuasive: For school events or awareness programs


The same content, rewritten in different tones, helps you match your communication style to your audience.


Collaboration and Sharing

Gamma is also collaborative.

  • You can share your deck with colleagues for feedback.

  • Real-time editing allows multiple educators to refine slides together.

  • Comment threads make it easy to discuss improvements.


This feature turns Gamma into a team workspace for educators, not just a personal tool.


Why Refining Matters

AI gives speed; humans give context. When you refine, you ensure the content:

  • Matches curriculum standards

  • Reflects your students’ understanding level

  • Aligns with your teaching objectives

 
 

You now know how to collaborate with Gamma like a digital co-teacher - editing, expanding, and polishing slides with ease.


But visual design also shapes how students receive information. In gamma you can also work on changing themes and layouts, restyle entire decks, adjust visuals, and make every slide look like it was made by a professional designer.

But visual design also shapes how students receive information. In gamma you can also work on changing themes and layouts, restyle entire decks, adjust visuals, and make every slide look like it was made by a professional designer.

Embedding Interactive Content

Static slides inform. Interactive slides inspire. In this video, we’ll explore how to make your presentations more engaging by embedding interactive elements. You’ll learn how to:

  • Add videos and links directly inside Gamma

  • Embed polls, quizzes, and forms

  • Encourage student participation and reflection


Interactivity transforms your presentations into experiences, making learning active, not passive.


Why Interactivity Matters

Students learn better when they can respond, click, and explore. Adding interactive elements like quizzes or videos improves:

  • Retention: Students remember concepts longer

  • Engagement: Learning feels participatory

  • Understanding: They connect visuals with real examples


Embedding Videos

Just paste a YouTube link or video URL. Gamma automatically embeds it with a playable preview - no technical setup needed.

Example: embedding a short NASA clip in your “Space Exploration” deck makes the topic instantly come alive.


Adding Polls and Quizzes

Gamma supports interactive embeds from tools like Mentimeter, Kahoot, and Typeform.  You can ask, “Which planet fascinates you most?” Students can respond live, and results appear instantly.


This creates active discussions and turns your presentation into a classroom event.


Linking Resources

You can embed:

  • PDFs

  • External articles

  • Online experiments or Google Docs


Students can click directly from your slide to explore further resources.


Sharing and Presenting

Once your deck is complete:

  • Share via link

  • Present live (like a webinar)

  • Export as a web page or PDF


Your presentation can live beyond the classroom, accessible anytime by your students or peers.

 
 

You’ve just unlocked the most exciting part of Gamma, turning slides into stories your students can interact with.


By now, you know how to create, edit, design, and enrich your presentations using AI.


Next, we’ll move beyond slides altogether and explore Canva Magic Studio; a creative AI platform that helps you design worksheets, infographics, posters, and classroom visuals effortlessly.


Get ready to take your visual storytelling to the next level.

Creating Web pages in Gamma

In this section, you will learn how to create your first web page using Gamma App, an intuitive tool for designing beautiful, interactive web pages without needing coding skills. This module will guide you step by step through the process of creating a career and internship counseling page designed for students and employers.


You’ll watch a demonstration video where we build a real example together and then try creating your own Gamma web page.

 
 

Beyond career and internship pages, there are many creative ways educators can use Gamma to enhance learning, communication, and engagement:

Use Case

Example Ideas

Course Information Pages

Create a page with syllabus, schedule, and links to resources.

Digital Portfolios

Showcase your professional achievements, teaching philosophy, and projects.

Student Projects

Let students create Gamma pages as part of assignments or presentations.

Event Pages

Promote upcoming workshops, career fairs, or department events.

Advising Resources

Share internship listings, career guides, or FAQs for students.

Parent/Community Updates

Create accessible pages with school news, highlights, or reports.

Creating Documents in Gamma

What Is a Gamma Document?

A Gamma document is more than just a regular file. It's a beautiful, interactive, and shareable digital page created effortlessly through AI assistance. Unlike traditional word processors or slide tools, Gamma automatically takes care of layout, design, and formatting, letting you focus purely on your ideas and content.


When you type, Gamma instantly transforms your text into a visually polished document with clean sections, clear headings, and engaging visuals.


Why Use Gamma for Document Creation?

Creating professional-looking documents has never been easier. Here’s why educators are turning to Gamma for academic and administrative communication:

  1. No formatting struggles

    No more adjusting margins, fonts, or bullet points. Gamma automatically formats your content into a beautiful layout.


  2. Fast and intuitive

    You can start from a blank page or a prompt (like “Create a placement guide for college students”) and Gamma instantly generates a draft. You can then edit, add sections, or redesign with a single click.


  3. AI-powered design

    Gamma suggests color themes, icons, and visuals to match your topic, giving every document a professional touch.


  4. Easy to share and update

    When your document is ready, simply share the link. You can edit anytime, and the shared version updates automatically. No re-sending files.


  5. Responsive and interactive

    Gamma documents adjust beautifully on phones, tablets, and desktops, ideal for today’s mobile learners.


In this video, you’ll see a demo of creating a placement guide in Gamma, a digital document that can include:

  • Tips for resume building

  • Interview preparation resources

  • Sample CV


With Gamma, you can create this guide in minutes, and make it visually appealing with sections, images, and color-coded content for easy navigation.


Other Ways Educators Can Use Gamma for Documents

Gamma isn’t just for placement guides. Educators are using it for many types of academic and administrative materials.

Document Type

Example Use

Course Handbooks

Present course structure, learning outcomes, and assessment methods.

Lesson Plans

Design interactive lesson outlines with embedded visuals or videos.

Research Summaries

Creating Documents in Gamma

What Is a Gamma Document?

A Gamma document is more than just a regular file. It's a beautiful, interactive, and shareable digital page created effortlessly through AI assistance. Unlike traditional word processors or slide tools, Gamma automatically takes care of layout, design, and formatting, letting you focus purely on your ideas and content.


When you type, Gamma instantly transforms your text into a visually polished document with clean sections, clear headings, and engaging visuals.


Why Use Gamma for Document Creation?

Creating professional-looking documents has never been easier. Here’s why educators are turning to Gamma for academic and administrative communication:

  1. No formatting struggles

    No more adjusting margins, fonts, or bullet points. Gamma automatically formats your content into a beautiful layout.


  2. Fast and intuitive

    You can start from a blank page or a prompt (like “Create a placement guide for college students”) and Gamma instantly generates a draft. You can then edit, add sections, or redesign with a single click.


  3. AI-powered design

    Gamma suggests color themes, icons, and visuals to match your topic, giving every document a professional touch.


  4. Easy to share and update

    When your document is ready, simply share the link. You can edit anytime, and the shared version updates automatically. No re-sending files.


  5. Responsive and interactive

    Gamma documents adjust beautifully on phones, tablets, and desktops, ideal for today’s mobile learners.

  • Interview preparation resources

  • Sample CV


With Gamma, you can create this guide in minutes, and make it visually appealing with sections, images, and color-coded content for easy navigation.

M3 - Task 1

Create a 10-Slide Presentation from a Single Prompt using Gamma

(Estimated time: 60 mins)


Objective: The goal of this task is to use Gamma to generate a professional, classroom-ready presentation, all from one well-written prompt. You will see how the clarity and structure of your prompt directly affect the quality of the slides that AI produces.


Instructions:

Choose Your Topic: Pick a subject or lesson idea you would genuinely teach. It could be from Science, Math, English, Social Studies, or even a co-curricular topic like teamwork or sustainability.


Write Your Prompt: Think carefully about what you want Gamma to create. Your prompt should include:

  • The topic (What the presentation is about)

  • The audience (Who it’s for – students, parents, or teachers)

  • The purpose (What you want learners to understand or do)

  • The format (e.g., “Create a 10-slide presentation”)


Example: “Create a 10-slide presentation for Grade 8 students explaining how climate change affects animal habitats. Keep the tone simple, visual, and engaging.”


Generate Your Slides: Open Gamma, enter your prompt, and let the AI create your presentation. Wait for the full deck to generate before making any edits.


Explore and Refine: Review the generated slides. Use Gamma’s tools to:

  • Edit the text or simplify the language.

  • Add or remove slides if needed.

  • Try changing themes or layouts to improve design.


    Add a Touch of Interactivity: If you want to go further, embed a YouTube video, quiz, or image link related to your topic.


    Review and Reflect: Look at your final 10-slide presentation. Ask yourself:

    • Does the flow make sense for my students?

    • Are the visuals clear and appealing?

    • Did the AI understand my prompt the way I expected?


Checklist & Self-Reflection:

Make sure you have completed the following steps in your own notes:

  1. Wrote your final prompt clearly and completely.

  2. Generated a full 10-slide presentation using Gamma.

  3. Reviewed and refined your slides for tone and accuracy.

  4. Added interactive or multimedia elements.


Self-Reflection Question: How did the wording of your prompt influence the final presentation design and content?


Once you’ve finished and reflected, mark this activity as ‘Complete’ in the LMS.

Whether you’re a teacher designing learning materials or a coordinator preparing school communication, Canva Magic Studio gives you the professional edge; no design degree required.

  • Magic Animate: Adds smooth motion and animation effects to your designs.


Each tool helps educators save time, enhance creativity, and build visually engaging materials.

The Magic Eraser Tool

Example:

Magic Grab is a tool that allows users to select and reposition individual objects within an image.


Example: Selecting a lamp and enlarging it to shift the visual focus.


Another example: Selecting a human figure (a woman) and flipping her horizontally to adjust composition.


This flexibility allows you to rearrange image components, change perspective, or adjust layout, without leaving Canva.

Creating Custom Classroom Visuals

Text-to-Image Generation

Educators often have a clear idea of what they want their students to see, but struggle to find the right image. Stock photo libraries might have something close, but not exactly what you need. 


Imagine wanting to show “college students presenting a marketing plan in a modern classroom,” but all you find online are generic, Western-centric images or unrelated visuals.


That’s where Text-to-Image generation changes everything.


In this section, you’ll learn:

  • What Text-to-Image AI is and how it works.

  • Why it’s a breakthrough tool for educators.

  • The types of visuals you can create, from creative illustrations to academic diagrams.

  • How you can use AI visuals in subjects like business, science, economics, and history.


By the end of this section, you’ll understand how to turn your teaching ideas into visuals that look like they were created by a professional designer, simply by describing them in words.


What Text-to-Image Generation Means

Text-to-Image generation is a process where AI converts written descriptions, known as “prompts”, into new, original visuals.


For example, when you type “a watercolor illustration of college students brainstorming ideas,” AI doesn’t search for an image online, it creates one from scratch using learned patterns of shapes, textures, and colors.


How It Works

When you write a prompt, the AI breaks down your words into key elements: subjects, actions, and context. It then visualizes them through digital rendering.


If your prompt says, “a modern classroom with students collaborating on laptops,” it understands “students,” “classroom,” and “laptops,” and then builds a coherent image combining those elements.


Why It’s a Game-Changer for Educators

Teachers can now instantly create classroom visuals, without design skills or expensive software.


You can generate:

  • Illustrations for storytelling or creative writing prompts.

  • Scientific diagrams for biology or chemistry.

  • Historical scenes for social studies.

  • Posters or infographics for business and management.


It saves time, reduces dependence on stock imagery, and allows personalization that reflects your students’ context; for example, creating visuals that show Indian students in modern learning environments.


Practical Classroom Examples

  • A business professor can generate a visual showing “a flowchart of product life cycle stages.”

  • A science teacher can create “a 3D-style diagram of the solar system labeled with planets.”

  • A history lecturer can request “a digital painting of India’s Independence movement in 1947.”

  • A communications instructor can generate “a group of students presenting ideas in a campus seminar.”


Ethical and Practical Considerations

AI-generated visuals are great for explanation and creativity, but teachers should ensure they are used ethically.


Avoid generating realistic portraits of people or sensitive content.


Prefer illustrations and diagrams for teaching, and always cross-check facts when using visuals for academic accuracy.

 
 

You’ve just discovered how Text-to-Image generation allows you to transform text into visuals instantly, helping you illustrate lessons, summarize ideas, and design more engaging materials.


Next, we’ll explore two powerful AI tools that make this process simple and practical: Canva’s Text to Image for creative and colorful visuals, and Microsoft Designer (via Copilot) for realistic, academic-quality images.


In the upcoming section, you’ll see how both tools work side-by-side, so you can choose the one that fits your teaching goals best.

Now that you understand what Text-to-Image generation is, let’s explore how to do it effectively.


You don’t need to install any software or learn design theory. AI tools like Canva and Microsoft Designer make the process visual, intuitive, and fun.


In this section, you’ll learn how to:

  • Access and use Canva’s Text to Image tool to generate illustrations and posters.

  • Create realistic visuals and diagrams using Microsoft Designer via Copilot.

  • Compare both tools to decide which one suits your classroom materials best.


Each has its own style. Canva is more creative and artistic, while Microsoft Designer is more realistic and structured. By mastering both, you’ll have every type of classroom visual at your fingertips.


Getting Started with Canva’s Text to Image

You open Canva, click Apps → Text to Image, and type your description.


For example:

“A watercolor illustration of college students collaborating on a project in a bright classroom.” Canva instantly generates several artistic versions of that image.


Choosing a Visual Style

Canva allows you to pick styles such as Photo, Drawing, Watercolor, or 3D Illustration. Each style serves a different educational purpose:

  • Photo: realistic for lesson recaps.

  • Drawing: great for worksheets.

  • Watercolor: ideal for story prompts.

  • 3D Illustration: perfect for modern classroom slides.


Using Microsoft Designer via Copilot

Now, we switch to Microsoft Designer. It works through the Copilot interface in Edge or Office 365.


Here, we can write more detailed prompts for realistic visuals, such as:

“A digital painting of the signing of India’s Constitution in 1949, realistic historical style.”

Or:

“An educational diagram of the human digestive system, labeled and clean background.”


Designer interprets these with depth and accuracy, great for academic visuals.


Comparing Canva vs. Microsoft Designer

Canva 

Microsoft Designer

Artistic and creative

Realistic and professional

Great for posters, slides, storytelling

Great for diagrams, history, and process visuals

Friendly interface, colorful tones

Detailed lighting and realism

Using both gives you flexibility. You can create everything from playful creative visuals to high-quality academic illustrations.


Practical Classroom Examples

  • Business Class: 

    • Canva → “Poster of 4Ps of Marketing.” 

    • Designer → “Realistic infographic of marketing funnel.”

  • History Lecture: 

    • Canva → “Cartoon of explorers trading goods.” 

    • Designer → “Historical painting of Mughal court.”

  • Science Lab: 

    • Canva → “Simplified chemistry lab illustration.” 

    • Designer → “Labeled 3D molecular diagram.”


Final Tips for Educators

  • Start with a simple prompt.

  • Try different styles to explore variety.

  • Use these visuals in presentations, digital notes, or e-learning portals.


You now know how to use Canva and Microsoft Designer to create visuals instantly, from artistic illustrations to realistic academic images.


The next step is learning how to control these results through better prompts.


In the next section, you’ll unlock the science behind writing strong AI prompts, the skill that helps you get exactly the image you want, every single time.


We’ll share the “Prompt Formula” and real classroom examples you can start using right away.

M3 - Quiz 3

Make it shorter so AI can “fill in the blanks.”
Remove adjectives to simplify the request.

Add details like “three diverse college students presenting a marketing plan in a bright, modern classroom, realistic photo style.”
